Stage 2: Ranking
Goal: score and rank the candidates from retrieval.
Method: a more expensive model (gradient-boosted tree or small neural network) with rich joint user-item features, recent interaction signals, contextual features (time of day, device, session behaviour).
Optimise for: precision and business metrics — not just click probability, but also revenue, margin, diversity.
Stage 3: Filtering
Goal: apply constraints the ML models do not know about.
Method: rule-based post-processing. Remove out-of-stock items. Apply legal restrictions. Enforce diversity. Apply business rules (boost promoted items). Deduplicate against recently shown items.
Updated by: operations teams without model retraining.
Architectural implication: the three stages have different scaling properties, different update cadences, and different ownership. Design them as separate, independently deployable components with separate SLAs.
Enterprise Example: Retail Group Personalised Promotion System
Scenario
A European retail group: 340 stores, 8 countries, 6.2 million loyalty members. They want personalised promotional offers — the right promotion, to the right customer, at the right time. Three business questions map to three AI problem types:
  • What to offer? Collaborative filtering — what items/promotions have similar customers responded to?
  • When to send it? Time series — when is this customer most likely to be in-market?
  • Which customers to target? Tabular prediction — which customers have high propensity to respond AND high lifetime value?
Collaborative Filtering — What to Offer
Two-Tower model on 18 months of purchase and promotion interaction history.

User tower: purchase category history, promotion response history, country, loyalty tier, demographic segment.

Item/promotion tower: product category, discount depth, promotion mechanics (BOGO vs % vs fixed), campaign creative type.

Customer embeddings pre-computed nightly. ANN retrieval finds 200 most relevant promotion candidates. LightGBM ranker scores each with contextual features (time since last purchase in category, inventory pressure, budget remaining).
Time Series — When to Send
LightGBM model on lag features of purchase history, predicting probability distribution of purchase timing.

Features: days since last purchase, historical inter-purchase interval (mean and variance), seasonal index for this customer's typical purchase categories, day-of-week purchase probability.

Output: a "send window" — a 7-day period within which the customer has the highest predicted probability of being in-market.
Tabular — Who to Target
Customer value score and campaign response propensity score computed daily for all active members using XGBoost.

Features: historical campaign response rate, average order value, purchase frequency trend (improving/stable/declining), loyalty tier, channel preference.

Only customers meeting both a minimum value score and a minimum propensity score threshold enter the promotion funnel.
PipelineDaily batch → compute customer embeddings → rank eligible customers → retrieve promotion candidates → rank candidates → apply business rules (budget, inventory, legal, recency) → generate personalised assignments → trigger communication platform
Click-through rate2.3× higher vs prior segment-based approach
Conversion rate1.8× higher
Promotional spend efficiency22% reduction in budget consumed per incremental sale
Architecture Perspective
Decomposition and Pipeline Design
  • Decompose multi-objective AI products first. Map business objectives to problem types (collaborative filtering, time series, tabular) before designing the architecture. Conflating them into a single model increases complexity without increasing capability.
  • The cascade pipeline is the only practical approach at scale. Attempting to score all user-item pairs at request time is computationally impossible beyond ~10K items. Design the three stages as independent microservices with separate SLAs, update cadences, and ownership.
  • Pre-computation is the key to recommendation latency. User and item embeddings should be pre-computed asynchronously. Anything requiring real-time computation over the full catalogue will not meet production latency requirements.
Data Discipline and Governance
  • Time series models require temporal train/test discipline. A common failure: the training lag feature is "sales yesterday" but the serving pipeline computes "sales at midnight on the day of serving" — not the same when serving happens at 2pm.
  • Cold start is a product problem, not just an ML problem. New users and new items require dedicated strategies: onboarding flows, content-based fallbacks, popularity-based bootstrapping. Architecture must include these fallback paths explicitly.
  • Feedback loop monitoring is non-negotiable. Log not just what was recommended and clicked, but also the counterfactual. Require an exploration strategy — occasionally surfacing non-top-ranked items. Without this, training data for future iterations is systematically biased by the current model.
Check Yourself
Five Architect Self-Test Questions
  1. Q: A PM asks for "an AI system that forecasts demand and personalises promotions." What architectural decomposition do you perform?
    Expected: Decompose into at least two distinct AI problem types. Demand forecasting = time series (exploits temporal patterns in sales history). Promotion personalisation = recommendation (collaborative filtering) + tabular prediction for response propensity. Different data requirements, model families, retraining cadences, and serving architectures. The decomposition also matters for ownership: demand forecasting often belongs to supply chain; promotion personalisation to marketing.
  2. Q: Retrieval is fast at 8ms for 2,000 candidates, but end-to-end latency is 450ms against a 150ms SLA. Where is the bottleneck?
    Expected: Almost certainly the ranking stage — it scores 2,000 candidates with rich features. Investigate: CPU vs GPU? Feature computation real-time vs pre-computed? Can retrieval return fewer candidates (e.g. 200 instead of 2,000, reducing ranking load 10×)? Can the ranking model be quantised or distilled? Also check whether the 450ms includes network round trips that could be parallelised or cached.
  3. Q: Collaborative filtering works well for your top 20% of customers but gives poor recommendations for the other 80% (infrequent purchasers). What is the problem, and what strategies address it?
    Expected: Cold start for sparse users. Strategies: (1) supplement user tower with content features available for all users regardless of purchase history; (2) fall back to popularity-based or content-based recommendations below an interaction threshold; (3) design onboarding flows to capture preference signals explicitly; (4) train a separate model for sparse users with different feature sets.
  4. Q: A data scientist proposes random cross-validation for the time series demand model. Why is this wrong?
    Expected: Random splits allow data from month 12 to appear in training while month 6 appears in test — the model "learns from the future." Correct approach: temporal (walk-forward) cross-validation — train on months 1–12, test on months 13–14; train on months 1–14, test on months 15–16; etc.
  5. Q: 8,000 new items go live on the website. How long until they appear in recommendations?
    Expected: Not until they have embeddings in the ANN index. Timeline = item ingestion time + embedding computation batch time + index rebuild time + serving tier cache refresh. For a daily batch pipeline: 12–24 hours. For a near-real-time pipeline: under an hour. The business must decide how much cold start exposure is acceptable and design the pipeline cadence accordingly.
Advanced Deep Dive
🟡 Optional depth — This section covers matrix factorisation vs two-tower architectures, Temporal Fusion Transformer internals, and recommendation metrics. Safe to skip on a first pass and return here when evaluating specific model architectures with your data science team.
8.1 Matrix Factorisation vs Two-Tower — Choosing Between Them
Matrix Factorisation
Trains directly on the interaction matrix. Uses only implicit feedback (clicks, purchases) or explicit ratings — no content features. Extremely fast to implement and effective when you have dense interaction data.

Cannot handle cold start — new users/items have no embeddings until they accumulate interactions.

Choose when: catalogue is relatively stable, users are mostly existing (low cold start pressure), team is small, and you need something working quickly.
Two-Tower
Strictly more flexible: uses content features, handles cold start better (item tower uses content even for new items), supports contextual signals (add a context tower for time of day, device, current session).

Trade-off: higher complexity — two separate neural networks to train, feature pipelines for both towers, more hyperparameters to tune.

Choose when: large or frequently changing catalogue, significant new user/item volume, contextual personalisation is important, or content features carry significant signal.
As an architect: The choice between MF and Two-Tower is primarily about cold start volume and catalogue churn. If your business has a stable catalogue and mature user base, MF is sufficient. If you are in retail with a constantly changing seasonal catalogue and significant new customer acquisition, Two-Tower is the right investment.
8.2 Temporal Fusion Transformer for Multi-Series Forecasting
Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)
A deep learning architecture specifically designed for multi-horizon forecasting with mixed input types. Unlike simple lag-feature models, TFT handles:

  • Multiple time series simultaneously — e.g. all SKUs across all stores
  • Static features per series — e.g. store size, product category (does not change over time)
  • Known future covariates — e.g. promotion schedule, holiday calendar (known in advance)
  • Unknown future covariates — e.g. past weather, sales (only known historically)

TFT uses LSTM layers (sequential processing), gated mechanisms (to select relevant temporal features), and multi-head attention (to capture long-range dependencies). It outputs quantile forecasts rather than point forecasts — a range of likely outcomes, critical for supply chain planning where you need to reason about risk.
As an architect: TFT is appropriate when the scale of the forecasting problem (thousands of series with rich covariates) justifies the investment. For simple demand forecasting with a small SKU set, LightGBM with lag features will perform comparably with a fraction of the operational complexity.
8.3 Recommendation Metrics
Quality Metrics
  • Precision@K: of the K items recommended, what fraction is relevant (clicked, purchased)? Measures quality of the top-K list.
  • Recall@K: of all items the user would eventually interact with, what fraction appeared in the top-K? Measures coverage of user preferences.
  • NDCG@K: Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain — measures whether relevant items appear near the top of the ranking, penalising relevant items lower in the list. Logarithmic penalty: a relevant item at position 1 is worth much more than at position 10.
System Health Metrics
  • Coverage: what fraction of all available items ever appear in recommendations? Low coverage = popularity feedback loop — the same popular items repeatedly recommended, the long tail never exposed.
  • Diversity: how different are the items in a single user's recommendation list? High diversity reduces filter bubble effects but is often traded off against precision.
  • Offline/online evaluation gap: offline metrics (precision@K on held-out interactions) often predict online performance (CTR in A/B test) poorly — historical interactions were shaped by the previous recommendation system. The only reliable evaluation is an online A/B test with randomised exposure.
Key Takeaways
  • Three distinct AI problem types: tabular prediction, time series forecasting, and collaborative filtering require different model families, different data structures, and different evaluation approaches. Decomposing a business request into the correct problem types before designing is the most important architectural move.
  • The cascade pipeline is canonical: retrieval → ranking → filtering partitions the problem into a fast approximate retrieval stage over millions of items and a slower, richer ranking stage over hundreds of candidates, making both quality and latency simultaneously achievable.
  • Time series models require temporal train/test splits without exception: random splits allow future data to leak into training and produce optimistic evaluation metrics that collapse in production.
  • Cold start is a structural limitation that must be designed around: new users and new items require dedicated fallback strategies (content-based, popularity-based, or onboarding flows) that are part of the system architecture, not edge cases.
  • Recommendation system feedback loops are a governance risk: without deliberate exploration (occasionally surfacing non-top-ranked items) and unbiased logging, training data for future iterations will be systematically shaped by the current model's biases, causing progressive narrowing of recommendations.
What Is It (Plain English)
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro — done at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going for architect-level detail.
Building an AI system and proving it works are two completely different problems. This module is about the second one.

It is surprisingly easy to build an AI system that looks like it is working. Your recommendation engine starts recommending things, customers buy some of them, and revenue is up. Is that the AI's contribution — or would those customers have bought those items anyway? Your chatbot resolves a percentage of tickets without human escalation. Are those resolutions actually satisfying customers, or are they just closing the ticket?

Data science — specifically causal inference, A/B testing, and uplift modelling — is the discipline that answers these questions. It provides the tools to distinguish between an AI system that is genuinely creating value and one that merely appears to be while the business outcome is driven by something else entirely.

The three central concepts:
  • Causal inference: understanding the difference between correlation (two things happening together) and causation (one thing making the other happen). This distinction is the difference between making the right decision and making the wrong decision based on correct-looking data.
  • A/B testing: the most reliable method for proving that an AI intervention caused an observed improvement, by randomly assigning users to conditions and measuring outcomes.
  • Uplift modelling: a technique for identifying which specific users benefit from an AI intervention, so that resources are targeted where they actually create value rather than deployed broadly on users who would have behaved the same way regardless.
Why Should I Care
For Solution Architects
You are often deciding whether to build or buy an AI system, and how to measure its success. Without the ability to distinguish correlation from causation, the success metrics you specify will be misleading.

Many architects accept vendor claims at face value: "Our recommendation engine increases conversion by 15%." But that 15%, unless measured with a randomised experiment against a control group, is almost certainly not 15% of causal lift. It may be 5% genuine improvement and 10% selection effect (the engine was showing up for customers who were already about to convert).

The A/B testing framework is infrastructure. A system that cannot run controlled experiments cannot learn whether it is working and cannot improve. Specifying the experiment assignment service, the logging, and the analysis pipeline is part of your architecture deliverable.
For Enterprise Architects
At the enterprise level, the stakes of confusing correlation with causation are high. An enterprise that believes its AI is adding 20% to revenue and invests accordingly — in licences, staff, infrastructure — based on a correlation rather than a causal measurement is misallocating capital. The causal measurement might show 5% genuine lift, not 20%.

This is not hypothetical. It is a pattern that plays out repeatedly in enterprise AI deployments. The algorithm recommends products to customers already in the purchase funnel. The reported lift is a mix of genuine improvement and statistical artefact.

Causal rigour in AI evaluation is also a regulatory and governance concern. Regulators in financial services and healthcare increasingly expect AI systems to demonstrate, not just claim, that their interventions cause the outcomes attributed to them.
Think About It Like This
Imagine a city installs smart traffic signals at an intersection. The following month, accidents drop by 30%. The mayor attributes the decline to the new signals.

But that same month: a new school opened nearby, reducing heavy vehicle traffic by 15%. It was an unusually dry month, with 40% fewer wet-road incidents. And a nearby construction project finished, removing a lane closure that had been causing erratic driving.

The 30% reduction is real. But how much was caused by the smart signals versus the school, the dry weather, and the end of construction? Without a controlled experiment — a similar intersection that did not get smart signals during the same period — you cannot separate the signal from the noise.

A/B testing is how you create the equivalent of that control intersection: randomly assign users to experience or not experience your AI system, hold everything else constant, and measure the difference. The randomisation is what creates causal validity — not the fanciness of the model.

Uplift modelling refines this further: not all drivers respond the same way to smart signals. Some would have driven carefully regardless. Uplift modelling finds the "persuadables" — the ones where the intervention actually makes a difference — and focuses resources on them.
Step-by-Step Walkthrough
4.1 Confounding: Why Correlation Fails
A confounder is a third variable that causes both the thing you are measuring as the "treatment" and the outcome you are trying to predict, creating the appearance of a causal relationship that does not exist.

Classic example: ice cream sales are highly correlated with drowning rates. Ice cream does not cause drowning. Summer heat (the confounder) causes both increased ice cream consumption and increased swimming, which leads to more drowning incidents.

Enterprise AI example: your recommendation system shows high correlation between "user received a recommendation" and "user made a purchase." But users who reach the recommendation widget are already deep in the purchase funnel — they have been browsing for 10 minutes, added items to their basket, and reached checkout. The purchase correlation is driven by purchase intent (the confounder), not by the recommendation's persuasive effect.
Common Confounders in Enterprise AI Evaluation
Selection-Based Confounders
  • Self-selection: users who opt into AI features (chatbots, recommendations, personalisation) are often higher-value users who behave differently regardless.
  • Recency: customers who have just made a purchase have different short-term behaviour; showing AI to recently active customers will always look like a strong correlation.
  • Survivorship: measuring outcomes only for users who completed a workflow ignores users who dropped out — and AI interventions often affect drop-out rates differently.
Temporal Confounders
  • Time of day / day of week: measurements at different times naturally have different conversion rates. If your AI runs on Tuesday and your baseline was measured on Saturday, the difference is not the AI's effect.
  • Seasonality: any measurement period may contain calendar effects (promotions, holidays, pay cycles) that were not present in the comparison period.

The only clean way to measure recommendation causal impact is to randomly withhold the recommendation from a portion of high-intent users and compare conversion rates.
4.2 A/B Testing: How You Prove AI ROI
An A/B test assigns users randomly to one of two conditions:
  • Control group (A): the baseline experience — no AI intervention, or the previous system.
  • Treatment group (B): the new AI intervention.
Because assignment is random, any differences between groups in user characteristics should be small and unbiased. The measured difference in outcome is therefore a reliable estimate of the causal effect of the AI intervention.
Unit of Randomisation
What entity is randomly assigned — the user, the session, the order, the store? The unit must match the level at which the intervention is applied. If your AI personalises at the user level, randomise at the user level. If it affects an entire store's operations, randomise at the store level (a "cluster randomised trial").
Sample Size and Power
Before running the test, calculate how many users you need in each group to detect a meaningful effect. This requires estimating the baseline conversion rate, the minimum effect size you care about, and the desired statistical power (typically 80–90%). Running a test with too few users and declaring victory based on a positive result is a form of false confidence — small samples produce noisy results that can be positive by chance.
Duration
Run the test for long enough to cover a full business cycle. A test run only on weekdays misses weekend effects. A test run over one week misses weekly patterns. For seasonally affected businesses, tests may need to run for weeks to months to avoid capturing a temporary seasonal effect as a permanent AI improvement.
Primary Metric
Define your primary metric before the test starts. Changing the primary metric after seeing results is p-hacking. Choose secondary guardrail metrics that catch unintended harms — a recommendation algorithm that increases clicks while decreasing customer satisfaction is harmful even if the click metric looks positive.
Multiple Testing
If you run many A/B tests simultaneously, some will be "positive" by chance. Apply Bonferroni correction (divide the significance threshold by the number of simultaneous tests) or use sequential testing methods that control the false discovery rate.
Novelty Effect
Users may behave differently because of the novelty of a new experience, not because of its genuine value. This inflates measured lift in the first few days or weeks. Run tests long enough for novelty to wear off.
4.3 Advanced A/B Testing Concepts
Statistical vs Practical Significance
A very large sample can make a tiny effect statistically significant. A 0.01% improvement in conversion that is statistically significant at p < 0.001 may not justify the operational cost of deploying the AI system. Always translate your statistical result into a business impact number before making a deployment decision.

Two-sided vs one-sided tests: a two-sided test asks "is there any difference between A and B?" Use two-sided tests in practice — you want to detect both positive and negative effects. Use one-sided tests only when there is no possibility B could be worse than A.
Sequential Testing and Long-Term Holdouts
Sequential testing: traditional A/B testing requires deciding sample size in advance and not looking at results until complete. Peeking early and stopping when positive inflates false positive rates. Sequential methods (CUPED, e-values, alpha-spending) allow checking results continuously without inflating false positive rates — important for fast-moving teams who cannot wait for a pre-specified sample size.

Holdout groups: some AI interventions have effects that only appear over long timeframes. Maintain permanent holdout groups (users who never receive the AI intervention) to measure long-term causal effects that short-term A/B tests cannot capture.
4.4 Uplift Modelling: Targeting AI Interventions
Standard A/B testing tells you the average effect of an AI intervention. But the average conceals enormous heterogeneity. For most interventions, users fall into four categories:
Persuadables (Target These)
Would not take the action without the intervention, but do take it with the intervention. These are the users where your AI creates genuine incremental value. Targeting them creates genuine incremental revenue.
Sure Things (Waste to Target)
Would take the action regardless of the intervention. Targeting them wastes AI resources and gives discounts to customers who would have paid full price — unnecessary interventions with no incremental value.
Lost Causes (Ignore)
Would not take the action regardless of the intervention. Targeting them wastes resources and achieves nothing.
Sleeping Dogs (Actively Avoid)
Would take the action without the intervention, but the intervention causes them to not take it — the AI contact backfires. Targeting them actively harms your metrics.
Standard ML models predict P(outcome = 1). Uplift models predict P(outcome = 1 | intervention) − P(outcome = 1 | no intervention) — the marginal effect of the intervention for each individual.

Building an uplift model requires data from an A/B test: the intervention assignment (treatment or control), the outcome, and features for prediction.
Two-Model Approach
Train one model on treatment group data and one model on control group data. The predicted uplift for a new user is the difference in their predicted outcomes from the two models.
Class Transformation
Transform the binary outcome using the treatment assignment to create a single transformed target. A single model trained on this target directly predicts uplift.
Meta-Learners
Families of approaches using flexible ML models to estimate treatment effects. X-Learner is the most robust for imbalanced treatment/control samples.
For architects: uplift modelling answers "which customers should we include in this intervention campaign?" (A/B testing answers "does this intervention work at all?"). The two are complementary. Run A/B tests to validate that an intervention works. Build uplift models to optimise who receives it.
Enterprise Example: Measuring AI-Powered Product Recommendation ROI
Scenario
A major European retail group deployed an AI product recommendation engine 6 months ago. The vendor claims a 14% revenue lift. The enterprise analytics team is tasked with verifying this claim and presenting findings to the CFO, who is deciding whether to renew a €2.4M annual contract.

The problem with the vendor's claim: the vendor measured "revenue from users who saw recommendations" vs "revenue from users who did not see recommendations." But users who reach the recommendation widget are deep in the checkout funnel — they have already added items to their basket. Users who did not see the widget may have left the site earlier. This is classic confounding by purchase intent.
Designing the Proper Measurement
Step 1: Randomised A/B Test
From all users who reach the recommendation widget, randomly assign 50% to see AI recommendations and 50% to see a static "You might also like" banner with no personalisation. This controls for purchase intent because both groups are equally deep in the funnel.
Step 2: Define Primary Metric
Before running the test: "incremental items added to basket from the recommendation area per session." Secondary (guardrail) metrics: average order value, return rate (does AI recommend items that get returned more?), customer satisfaction score from post-purchase surveys.
Step 3: Calculate Sample Size
At a baseline add-to-basket rate of 8%, to detect a 1% absolute improvement (12.5% relative lift) with 80% power and 5% significance level: approximately 58,000 sessions per group. At current traffic: 3 weeks to reach this.
Step 4: Run for Full Business Cycle
Run for 4 weeks (to cover a full monthly cycle, including the mid-month payday pattern in customer behaviour).
Results
Treatment (AI recommendations)Add-to-basket rate = 9.1%
Control (static banner)Add-to-basket rate = 8.3%
Absolute lift0.8 percentage points (10% relative lift)
Statistical significancep = 0.003 (well below 0.05 threshold)
Average order valueNo statistically significant difference
Return rateNo statistically significant difference
Reframing the ROI: the vendor claimed 14% revenue lift. The actual causal lift on the measured metric is 10% relative improvement in add-to-basket rate from the recommendation area. The recommendation area accounts for approximately 6% of total revenue. Therefore, the causal revenue lift is approximately 10% × 6% = 0.6% of total revenue, not 14%.
Uplift Analysis Add-On
The analytics team builds an uplift model on the A/B test data to find which customers have the highest incremental response. Finding: new customers (fewer than 3 orders in the last 12 months) and customers in specific category segments (home and kitchen, sports equipment) have 3–4× higher uplift than the average.

Showing AI recommendations to these segments specifically would achieve 80% of the total revenue lift while reducing sessions served by the recommendation engine by 40% — a significant infrastructure cost saving.
Governance outcomeCFO receives a methodologically sound analysis correcting the vendor's claim
Contract decisionRenew but negotiate on verified 0.6% revenue lift basis, not vendor's 14% claim
Negotiation saving€400K
Additional savingInfrastructure cost reduction via uplift-based targeting strategy
Architecture Perspective
Experiment Infrastructure as Core Component
  • Experiment infrastructure is not optional reporting. An AI system without the ability to run controlled experiments cannot learn whether it is working, cannot improve, and cannot be held accountable. Specify the experiment assignment service, event logging pipeline, and analysis pipeline in your architecture design — before launch.
  • Causal metrics require logging most AI platforms do not provide by default. To measure causal impact, you need to log: which experiment variant each user was assigned to, what intervention they received (or did not receive), and what outcome occurred. Many AI vendor platforms log only what was recommended and what was clicked — not the counterfactual. This is a due-diligence question to ask vendors before purchase.
Governance and Long-Term Measurement
  • Guardrail metrics prevent optimising for the wrong thing. Specify metrics that must not get worse — customer satisfaction score, return rate, customer service contact rate, long-term retention rate. If any guardrail degrades significantly, the AI intervention fails even if the primary metric improves.
  • The "holdout forever" pattern for long-term measurement: maintain a permanent holdout group (typically 5–10% of users) who never receive AI interventions. This allows you to measure the cumulative effect of AI personalisation over months or years. Without this, you can never measure effects that take longer than a single A/B test to manifest.
  • Uplift model outputs must be operationalised at the campaign planning level. This requires integration between the data science platform (where uplift scores are generated) and the campaign management system (where targeting decisions are made). This integration is an architecture deliverable.
Check Yourself
Five Architect Self-Test Questions
  1. Q: A stakeholder says "Our AI-powered email campaign drove a 23% higher open rate than last month's campaign. The AI is clearly working." What questions do you ask?
    Expected: Was there a control group — a group who received a non-AI email during the same period? Were the two groups randomly assigned, or did the AI target more engaged users? What changed between the two campaigns beyond the AI personalisation (offer, creative, send time, list)? Is a 23% higher open rate statistically significant given the sample sizes? A proper A/B test with random assignment and pre-determined sample size is the only way to make a defensible causal claim.
  2. Q: After 2 weeks, a chatbot treatment group shows 15% lower contact rate than control, p = 0.04. The PM declares success. What concerns do you raise?
    Expected: "Lower contact rate" is not "better customer service" — customers who give up also reduce contact rate. Measure resolution rate, customer satisfaction scores, and downstream outcomes (higher cancellation rates?). Two weeks may be too short for novelty effect to wear off. p = 0.04 is marginal — was this significance threshold pre-specified? Are there segment effects where specific groups (elderly customers, complex issues, non-native speakers) are experiencing significantly worse outcomes?
  3. Q: Explain the difference between a "sure thing" and a "persuadable" in uplift modelling, and why this distinction matters for a retail promotion AI system.
    Expected: A "sure thing" converts regardless of the promotion — targeting them gives discounts to customers who would have paid full price without creating incremental revenue. A "persuadable" converts only because of the promotion — targeting them creates genuine incremental revenue. Uplift modelling identifies persuadables by estimating the marginal effect of the promotion for each individual, improving the efficiency of the promotion budget by 30–50% in typical enterprise deployments.
  4. Q: What is a confounding variable? Give a specific example from a customer data context where a confounder would cause an AI system to appear to be working when it is not.
    Expected: A confounding variable causes both the treatment (exposure to the AI intervention) and the outcome, creating the appearance of a causal relationship when none exists. Example: a customer retention AI sends re-engagement emails to customers who have not purchased in 60 days, evaluated by measuring purchase rate among customers who opened the email vs those who did not. Email open propensity is the confounder — customers who open emails are already more engaged and likely to return regardless. A correct evaluation would randomly assign 60-day dormant customers to receive or not receive the email, and compare purchase rates between the two groups.
  5. Q: The product team says the AI recommendation engine drove €4.2M in recommendations revenue last quarter. How do you translate this into a causal revenue attribution figure?
    Expected: The €4.2M is "revenue from purchases that included a recommended item" — not the revenue caused by the recommendation. The causal attribution requires a two-step calculation: (1) from your A/B test results, find the incremental conversion rate lift the AI causes over the control condition (e.g. a 10% relative improvement in add-to-basket rate); (2) apply this lift to the portion of revenue the recommendation area influences (e.g. 6%). Causal attribution = 10% × 6% × total revenue. On a quarterly revenue base of €70M, that is 0.6% × €70M = €420K of genuinely incremental revenue — one-tenth of the €4.2M figure.
Advanced Deep Dive
🟡 Optional depth — This section covers quasi-experimental methods (DiD, RDD, instrumental variables) and advanced uplift modelling techniques. Safe to skip on a first pass; return here when your team needs to evaluate causal claims in complex rollout scenarios.
8.1 Causal Inference Beyond A/B Tests
Pure randomised A/B tests are not always possible. Sometimes you cannot randomise — regulatory constraints, technical limitations, or ethical concerns may prevent withholding a feature from some users. Causal inference provides quasi-experimental alternatives.
Quasi-Experimental Methods
  • Difference-in-Differences (DiD): compare the change in outcome over time between a group that received the treatment and a group that did not. If both groups were trending similarly before treatment (the "parallel trends" assumption), the divergence after treatment estimates the causal effect. Used when the treatment rolled out to some stores/regions but not others.
  • Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD): exploit a threshold in an assignment rule. If customers above loyalty tier threshold X receive the AI feature and those just below do not, compare outcomes for customers just above and just below the threshold. Near the threshold, assignment is quasi-random.
Further Methods
  • Instrumental Variables (IV): use a third variable (the "instrument") that affects treatment but has no direct effect on the outcome other than through the treatment. Example: if a software bug randomly prevented some users from seeing the AI feature, the bug exposure can be used as an instrument.
  • Propensity Score Matching: for observational data with no randomisation, match treated and control users who have similar propensity to receive the treatment. The matched groups can then be compared. Weaker than randomisation but stronger than no adjustment — controls for observed confounders but cannot control for unobserved ones.
As an architect: You would specify the need for quasi-experimental methods when a clean A/B test is impossible (e.g. a regional rollout, a policy change that affects everyone). Your data science team would select and implement the specific method.
8.2 Uplift Modelling with the X-Learner
X-Learner: Handling Imbalanced Treatment Groups
The X-Learner is a meta-learner for uplift modelling that handles the common situation where treatment and control groups are of different sizes (or where base outcome rates differ substantially between groups).

  1. Step 1: Train two outcome models — one on the treatment group (predicting outcome given treatment) and one on the control group (predicting outcome given no treatment).
  2. Step 2: Use the control outcome model to impute what treatment group members would have done without treatment. Use the treatment outcome model to impute what control group members would have done with treatment. This creates imputed individual treatment effects for both groups.
  3. Step 3: Train two uplift models — one on the treatment group's imputed effects and one on the control group's imputed effects.
  4. Step 4: Combine the two uplift estimates using a weighting function (often based on propensity scores) to produce a final uplift estimate for each individual.

The X-Learner is preferred over simpler two-model approaches when the treatment and control groups are unbalanced — for example, in a holdout A/B test where 90% received treatment and 10% were in control.
As an architect: You encounter X-Learner when your data science team reports that treatment and control groups are very different in size or composition. Your role is to understand why a more complex method was needed, not to implement it.
8.3 Evaluating Uplift Models
Unlike classification models (which have clear accuracy metrics), uplift models are harder to evaluate because the true individual treatment effect is never observed — you can only see what happened under one condition for each user.
Qini Coefficient
Measures the cumulative uplift captured as you target progressively more users in order of their predicted uplift score. A perfect uplift model immediately captures all true persuadables. A random model captures them proportionally. The Qini coefficient measures the area between the uplift model curve and the random baseline.
Uplift Curve / Cumulative Gain Chart
Plots cumulative incremental outcomes as you target progressively more users by decreasing predicted uplift score. Use this to decide the optimal targeting threshold — the point where marginal uplift equals the cost of the intervention.
Uplift@K
At the top K% of users by predicted uplift, what is the measured incremental outcome compared to a random sample of the same size? Requires either a holdout experiment or a two-stage A/B test where both treated and untreated users are randomly sampled.
As an architect: When a data science team presents a Qini curve or Uplift@K metric to justify a campaign management system, you now have the vocabulary to ask the right questions: what is the targeting economics, and how much of the benefit comes from the persuadable segment?
Key Takeaways
  • Correlation is not causation, and the difference matters financially: an AI system that appears to drive 14% revenue lift based on correlational measurement may be driving 0.6% causal lift once confounders are removed — a 20× difference that determines whether the system is worth its cost.
  • A/B testing requires four non-negotiable design elements: random assignment, pre-specified primary metric, adequate sample size calculated before the test runs, and sufficient duration to cover a full business cycle.
  • Guardrail metrics must be specified alongside success metrics: an AI system that improves conversion while degrading customer satisfaction, increasing return rates, or reducing long-term retention causes net harm even if its primary metric looks positive.
  • Uplift modelling identifies which specific users benefit from an AI intervention: enabling targeted deployment that achieves 70–80% of the value at 30–50% of the cost by avoiding "sure things" who would have converted anyway and "sleeping dogs" who are actively harmed by the intervention.
  • Experiment infrastructure must be specified as a core component before launch: the assignment service, event logging pipeline, and analysis tooling are not optional reporting tools — an AI system that cannot run controlled experiments cannot prove its own value or improve over time.
Live coding interviews are the highest-stakes component for ML/AI engineering roles. FAANG and research labs expect complete implementations in 20-30 minutes no libraries, clean code, explained as you go. This tab contains 8 core implementations (Multi-Head Attention, Backprop 2-layer net, K-Means, BPE, VAE, Transformer block, LSTM, Beam search) with complete working code, exactly what interviewers probe, and common follow-up questions.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

This tab is your live coding interview cheat sheet for AI / ML roles. The pattern is the same at every serious AI company: someone shares a code editor, you have 20-30 minutes, and you have to implement a classic ML building block from scratch — no LangChain, no high-level PyTorch shortcuts, just NumPy or raw PyTorch primitives. They want to see clean code, correct math, and you explaining what you are doing as you go.

Why this matters even if you do not love coding interviews: there is no faking your way through implementing Multi-Head Attention or backprop. Either you understand it well enough to write it, or you do not. Interviewers use this round precisely because it cannot be cheated by reading model cards or repeating buzzwords. Once you have implemented the core 8 algorithms a few times, future interviews stop being scary — you just type out what is already in your fingers.

What this tab covers: the 8 implementations that come up again and again — Multi-Head Attention, backprop for a 2-layer network, K-Means clustering, BPE tokenisation, VAE, a full Transformer block, LSTM, and beam search. For each one: complete working code, what interviewers probe (“why this and not that?”), and the common follow-up questions.

1. Multi-Head Attention (FAANG Standard)
Complete Implementation
PyTorch Multi-Head Attention from scratch
import torch, math import torch.nn.functional as F import torch.nn as nn class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d_model=512, n_heads=8, dropout=0.1): super().__init__() assert d_model % n_heads == 0 self.d_k = d_model // n_heads self.n_heads = n_heads # No bias in modern LLMs (matches LLaMA 3 architecture) self.Wq = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wk = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wv = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wo = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout) def forward(self, x, mask=None): B, T, C = x.shape # Project and reshape: (B, T, C) (B, h, T, d_k) Q = self.Wq(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) K = self.Wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) V = self.Wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) # Scaled dot-product attention scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k) if mask is not None: scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf")) attn = self.drop(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)) out = (attn @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C) return self.Wo(out) def causal_mask(T, device): "Upper triangular mask for autoregressive decoding." return torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T, device=device)).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0) # Test mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=256, n_heads=8) x = torch.randn(2, 10, 256) # (batch, seq, d_model) mask = causal_mask(10, x.device) out = mha(x, mask) print(out.shape) # torch.Size([2, 10, 256])
Key Interview Questions
  • Why sqrt(d_k) scaling? QK^T has variance d_k (if Q,K ~ N(0,1)). Large d_k large scores softmax saturates near-zero gradients. Dividing by d_k variance = 1 stable gradients.
  • Why multi-head? Different heads attend to different relationship types (syntax, semantics, coreference). Learned implicitly from data. Single head = bottleneck on what relationships can be captured simultaneously.
  • Implement KV cache: Keep dict of past K,V. New token: compute Q only (from new token), compute K,V for new token, append to cache, run attention over all past K,V. Amortizes the O(T) cost across generation steps.
  • Flash Attention vs standard: Standard materializes full TT matrix in HBM (slow). Flash: tiles into SRAM (fast), online softmax, never stores full matrix. Same output. 2-4 faster, O(T) vs O(T) memory.
2. Backpropagation from Scratch
2-Layer Network with Full Derivation
NumPy Backprop from scratch
import numpy as np class TwoLayerNet: def __init__(self, d_in, d_h, d_out, lr=0.001): # He init: std = sqrt(2/fan_in) for ReLU self.W1 = np.random.randn(d_in, d_h) * np.sqrt(2 / d_in) self.b1 = np.zeros(d_h) self.W2 = np.random.randn(d_h, d_out) * np.sqrt(2 / d_h) self.b2 = np.zeros(d_out) self.lr = lr def _softmax(self, z): e = np.exp(z - z.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)) # numerical stability return e / e.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) def forward(self, X): self.X = X self.z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1 self.a1 = np.maximum(0, self.z1) # ReLU self.z2 = self.a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2 self.p = self._softmax(self.z2) return self.p def backward(self, y_onehot): N = y_onehot.shape[0] # dL/dz2 = p - y_true (softmax+CE combined gradient) dz2 = (self.p - y_onehot) / N dW2 = self.a1.T @ dz2 db2 = dz2.sum(axis=0) # Backprop through ReLU: gradient = 1 if z1>0 else 0 da1 = dz2 @ self.W2.T dz1 = da1 * (self.z1 > 0) dW1 = self.X.T @ dz1 db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0) # SGD update self.W2 -= self.lr * dW2; self.b2 -= self.lr * db2 self.W1 -= self.lr * dW1; self.b1 -= self.lr * db1 loss = -(y_onehot * np.log(self.p + 1e-12)).sum(axis=1).mean() return loss
Why the Softmax+CE Gradient is Clean
The softmax + cross-entropy gradient is the most important result in deep learning and it comes out remarkably clean despite looking complex at first.
Setup: z = logits (raw model outputs, one per class) p_k = softmax(z)_k = exp(z_k) / sum_j exp(z_j) (probabilities) y_k = true label (1 for correct class, 0 for all others) L = -sum_k y_k * log(p_k) (cross-entropy loss) Goal: compute dL/dz_k (gradient of loss w.r.t. each logit) Derivation: dL/dz_k = sum_j (-y_j / p_j) * dp_j/dz_k Softmax self-derivative: dp_k/dz_k = p_k * (1 - p_k) Softmax cross-derivative: dp_j/dz_k = -p_j * p_k (for j != k) Substituting: dL/dz_k = -(y_k/p_k) * p_k*(1-p_k) + sum_{j!=k} (y_j/p_j) * p_j*p_k = -y_k*(1-p_k) + sum_{j!=k} y_j * p_k = -y_k + p_k * sum_j y_j = p_k - y_k (since sum_j y_j = 1 for one-hot labels!) Final result: dL/dz_k = p_k - y_k
p_kpredicted probability for class k after softmax. Between 0 and 1
y_ktrue label for class k. 1 if this is the correct class, 0 for all others (one-hot encoding)
dL/dz_k = p_k - y_kthe remarkably clean result: gradient is simply 'prediction minus truth'. Positive = model over-predicted (push down). Negative = model under-predicted (push up)
dp_k/dz_k = p_k(1-p_k)softmax self-derivative: sigmoid-like, but comes from the normalisation
dp_j/dz_k = -p_j*p_kcross-derivative: softmax at position j also depends on z_k through the normalisation sum. Negative because increasing z_k reduces all other probabilities
sum_j y_j = 1for one-hot labels: exactly one class has y=1, all others have y=0. This is what makes the sum collapse so cleanly
Key insight: This clean gradient (p_k - y_k) is why cross-entropy + softmax is the universal choice for classification. There is no saturation, the gradient is always non-zero when the prediction is wrong, and it has a beautiful intuitive interpretation: 'push the correct class up, push all others down, proportionally to how wrong you were'.
Remember it as: dL/dz = p - y = 'predicted minus actual'. If you predicted 0.3 for the right class but truth is 1.0: gradient is -0.7 -> push that logit up. If you predicted 0.2 for a wrong class: gradient is +0.2 -> push that logit down. Exactly what you'd want.
Live Coding Interview What Each Implementation Tests
ImplementationWhat It TestsKey Concepts to Explain While CodingCommon Follow-up
Multi-Head AttentionCore transformer understanding, tensor operationsWhy sqrt(d_k)? What does the mask do? Why multi-head?How would you add KV cache? Implement Flash Attention?
Backpropagation 2-layer netChain rule, gradient flow, understanding of NN trainingWhy is dL/dz = py so clean? What is the ReLU gradient?How would you add L2 regularisation? What causes vanishing gradients?
K-Means with K-Means++Clustering intuition, algorithmic thinking, vectorisationWhy K-Means++ over random init? How to choose k?How would you scale to 100M points? When does K-Means fail?
BPE TokenizerNLP fundamentals, text processing, algorithm designWhy subword over character/word? What is the fertility problem?How does SentencePiece differ? What is byte-level BPE?
LSTM from scratchSequential models, gating mechanisms, memoryWhat does each gate do? How does it solve vanishing gradients?Why did transformers replace LSTMs? When would you still use an LSTM?
Beam SearchDecoding strategies, search algorithms, NLP generationWhy not greedy? What is the length normalisation for?When does beam search fail? What is nucleus sampling?
VAE (encoder + decoder)Generative models, reparameterization trick, ELBO lossWhy reparameterize? What does the KL term enforce? Why not just autoencoder?How does -VAE differ? When use VAE vs diffusion vs GAN?
Transformer blockEnd-to-end transformer wiring, pre-norm vs post-norm, residualsWhy pre-norm? Why residuals around each sub-layer? Why SwiGLU FFN?How does this become a full decoder LM? Where do RoPE / KV cache plug in?
Interview Coding Framework 5 Steps Every Time
1
Clarify before writing (30 seconds)
"Should I use NumPy or pure Python?" "Should this handle batches?" "What precision?" This signals architect thinking, not just implementation.
2
State the algorithm in one sentence
Before any code: "K-Means repeatedly assigns points to nearest centroid then recomputes centroids until convergence." This proves you understand the concept, not just the code.
3
Write clean code with comments on the WHY
Comment non-obvious choices: "# subtract max for numerical stability" on softmax. "# B=zeros so DeltaW=0 at init, preserving pretrained weights" on LoRA. Interviewers test understanding, not typing speed.
4
Test with a simple example, narrate the output
"With input [1,2,3], softmax gives [0.09, 0.24, 0.67]. Highest input highest probability. Sum = 1.0 as expected." Running through a concrete example live shows you can debug.
5
Discuss complexity and real-world considerations
"This is O(nkditerations). In production I'd use scikit-learn's MiniBatchKMeans for 100M points, or FAISS for vector clustering. The main limitation is the assumption of spherical clusters."
3. K-Means with K-Means++ Init
Full Implementation
NumPy — K-Means clustering from scratch
import numpy as np def kmeans(X, k, max_iter=300, tol=1e-4, seed=42): np.random.seed(seed) n, d = X.shape # K-Means++ initialization centroids = [X[np.random.randint(n)]] for _ in range(k - 1): # Distance to nearest centroid (squared) D2 = np.array([min(np.linalg.norm(x - c)**2 for c in centroids) for x in X]) # Sample proportional to D spreads centroids out probs = D2 / D2.sum() centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(n, p=probs)]) centroids = np.array(centroids) for _ in range(max_iter): # Vectorized: (n,1,d) - (1,k,d) (n,k) distances dists = np.linalg.norm(X[:, None] - centroids[None], axis=2) labels = dists.argmin(axis=1) new_c = np.array([X[labels == i].mean(axis=0) if (labels == i).any() else centroids[i] for i in range(k)]) if np.linalg.norm(new_c - centroids) < tol: break centroids = new_c inertia = sum(np.linalg.norm(X[labels==i] - centroids[i])**2 for i in range(k)) return labels, centroids, inertia
More Essential Implementations
NumPy Softmax, cosine similarity, precision@k
import numpy as np def softmax(z): "Numerically stable softmax." e = np.exp(z - z.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)) return e / e.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True) def cosine_similarity(a, b): "Cosine similarity, handles batches." a_n = a / (np.linalg.norm(a, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8) b_n = b / (np.linalg.norm(b, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8) return (a_n * b_n).sum(axis=-1) def precision_at_k(relevant, retrieved, k): "Precision@K for information retrieval." top_k = set(retrieved[:k]) return len(top_k & set(relevant)) / k def cross_entropy(logits, y_true): "Cross-entropy loss with integer class labels." N = len(y_true) p = softmax(logits) return -np.log(p[np.arange(N), y_true] + 1e-12).mean() def rmsNorm(x, gamma, eps=1e-8): "RMS normalization (used in LLaMA, Mistral)." rms = np.sqrt((x**2).mean(axis=-1, keepdims=True) + eps) return gamma * x / rms
More Critical Implementations
LSTM from Scratch
Python LSTM cell implementation
import numpy as np class LSTMCell: def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size): self.h = hidden_size # Xavier init for all 4 gate weight matrices stacked scale = np.sqrt(2.0 / (input_size + hidden_size)) self.W = np.random.randn(4*hidden_size, input_size+hidden_size)*scale self.b = np.zeros(4*hidden_size) def sigmoid(self, x): return 1/(1+np.exp(-x)) def forward(self, x_t, h_prev, c_prev): "One LSTM step. Returns (h_t, c_t)." xh = np.concatenate([x_t, h_prev]) gates = self.W @ xh + self.b # (4h,) all gates i = self.sigmoid(gates[:self.h]) # input gate f = self.sigmoid(gates[self.h:2*self.h]) # forget gate g = np.tanh(gates[2*self.h:3*self.h]) # cell gate o = self.sigmoid(gates[3*self.h:]) # output gate c_t = f * c_prev + i * g # cell state h_t = o * np.tanh(c_t) # hidden state return h_t, c_t
Beam Search
Python Beam search for text generation
import heapq def beam_search(model, start_token, beam_width=5, max_len=50, eos_token=2): "Beam search for autoregressive generation." # Beam: (neg_log_prob, token_sequence) beams = [(0.0, [start_token])] completed = [] for _ in range(max_len): candidates = [] for score, seq in beams: if seq[-1] == eos_token: completed.append((score, seq)); continue # Get next token probabilities from model log_probs = model.next_token_log_probs(seq) # (vocab,) # Take top beam_width candidates top_k = log_probs.argsort()[-beam_width:][::-1] for token in top_k: candidates.append( (score - log_probs[token], seq + [token])) # Keep top beam_width beams (min-heap on neg log prob) beams = heapq.nsmallest(beam_width, candidates, key=lambda x: x[0]) if not beams: break completed.extend(beams) best = min(completed, key=lambda x: x[0]/len(x[1])) # length-norm return best[1]
4. BPE Tokenizer (GPT / Llama tokeniser core)
BPE Training Loop
Python — Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch
from collections import Counter, defaultdict def get_pair_counts(corpus): "corpus = dict of {tuple_of_symbols: freq}. Returns Counter of adjacent pairs." pairs = Counter() for symbols, freq in corpus.items(): for i in range(len(symbols) - 1): pairs[(symbols[i], symbols[i+1])] += freq return pairs def merge_pair(corpus, pair): "Replace every occurrence of `pair` with its concatenation in every word." a, b = pair merged = a + b new_corpus = {} for symbols, freq in corpus.items(): out, i = [], 0 while i < len(symbols): if i < len(symbols)-1 and symbols[i]==a and symbols[i+1]==b: out.append(merged); i += 2 else: out.append(symbols[i]); i += 1 new_corpus[tuple(out)] = freq return new_corpus def train_bpe(words, n_merges=10000): "words = list of strings. Returns merge rules in learned order." # Each word starts as a tuple of single chars + end-of-word marker corpus = Counter(tuple(list(w) + ['</w>']) for w in words) merges = [] for _ in range(n_merges): pairs = get_pair_counts(corpus) if not pairs: break best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get) # most frequent pair corpus = merge_pair(corpus, best) merges.append(best) return merges def encode(word, merges): "Apply learned merges greedily to a new word." symbols = list(word) + ['</w>'] for a, b in merges: i = 0 while i < len(symbols)-1: if symbols[i]==a and symbols[i+1]==b: symbols = symbols[:i] + [a+b] + symbols[i+2:] else: i += 1 return symbols
Why BPE wins
Vocab controlFixed budget (50K). Char-level too long, word-level explodes & OOVs. BPE is the sweet spot.
No OOVAny UTF-8 byte sequence decomposes into known sub-tokens. Byte-level BPE (GPT-2+) handles emoji, code, every language.
Compression-drivenMost frequent pair merged first → common English words become single tokens, rare ones split. Average ~4 chars/token for English.
FertilityTokens/word ratio. English ~1.3, Hindi ~2.5, code ~1.8. Lower = cheaper inference per language.
Interview hook: “Walk me through how GPT tokenises ‘tokenization’.” Answer: starts as 12 chars + </w>, greedily applies merges in learned order, ends as ~3 tokens (‘token’, ‘ization’, </w>). Show the merge trace.
5. VAE — Variational Autoencoder (encoder + decoder + ELBO)
PyTorch implementation
PyTorch — minimal VAE with reparameterisation trick
import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class VAE(nn.Module): def __init__(self, in_dim=784, hidden=400, z_dim=20): super().__init__() # Encoder: x -> (mu, logvar) parameterising q(z|x) self.enc_fc = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden) self.enc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim) self.enc_logvar = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim) # Decoder: z -> x_hat parameterising p(x|z) self.dec_fc1 = nn.Linear(z_dim, hidden) self.dec_fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden, in_dim) def encode(self, x): h = F.relu(self.enc_fc(x)) return self.enc_mu(h), self.enc_logvar(h) def reparameterise(self, mu, logvar): # Trick: z = mu + sigma * eps. Gradient flows through mu & logvar; # randomness lives in eps which is detached from the graph. std = torch.exp(0.5 * logvar) eps = torch.randn_like(std) return mu + eps * std def decode(self, z): h = F.relu(self.dec_fc1(z)) return torch.sigmoid(self.dec_fc2(h)) def forward(self, x): mu, logvar = self.encode(x.view(x.size(0), -1)) z = self.reparameterise(mu, logvar) return self.decode(z), mu, logvar def vae_loss(x_hat, x, mu, logvar): # Reconstruction: how well decoder recovers x from z recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x.view(x.size(0), -1), reduction='sum') # KL(N(mu, sigma^2) || N(0, I)) = 0.5 * sum(mu^2 + sigma^2 - log sigma^2 - 1) kl = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + logvar - mu.pow(2) - logvar.exp()) return recon + kl # -ELBO
Why this matters
Reparam trickWithout it, sampling z breaks backprop (gradient cannot flow through a stochastic node). With z = μ + σ·ε, randomness is in ε only — gradient flows through μ & σ.
ELBOlog p(x) ≥ E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x) || p(z)). Maximise lower bound = reconstruction - KL.
KL = regulariserForces q(z|x) toward N(0,I) prior → latent space is smooth & samplable. Without it: plain autoencoder, no generation.
vs diffusionVAE: one-shot sample, sharper-but-blurrier images, fast. Diffusion: iterative denoising, higher fidelity, 25-50 steps.
Follow-up trap: “Why not just maximise log p(x) directly?” — because log p(x) = log ∫ p(x|z) p(z) dz is intractable for any non-trivial decoder. ELBO is the tractable surrogate; KL gap closes as encoder gets better.
6. Transformer Block (pre-norm, residuals, MHA + SwiGLU FFN)
PyTorch implementation
PyTorch — modern pre-norm transformer block (Llama-style)
import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class RMSNorm(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, eps=1e-6): super().__init__() self.g = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(d)) self.eps = eps def forward(self, x): return self.g * x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps) class SwiGLU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, hidden): super().__init__() self.w1 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False) # gate self.w2 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False) # up self.w3 = nn.Linear(hidden, d, bias=False) # down def forward(self, x): return self.w3(F.silu(self.w1(x)) * self.w2(x)) class MHA(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, n_heads, causal=True): super().__init__() assert d % n_heads == 0 self.h, self.dh, self.causal = n_heads, d // n_heads, causal self.qkv = nn.Linear(d, 3 * d, bias=False) self.proj = nn.Linear(d, d, bias=False) def forward(self, x): B, T, D = x.shape q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(D, dim=-1) q = q.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) # (B,h,T,dh) k = k.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) v = v.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) # Uses Flash Attention under the hood when on CUDA/H100 out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=self.causal) out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, D) return self.proj(out) class TransformerBlock(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, n_heads, ffn_hidden): super().__init__() self.attn_norm = RMSNorm(d) # pre-norm before attention self.attn = MHA(d, n_heads) self.ffn_norm = RMSNorm(d) # pre-norm before FFN self.ffn = SwiGLU(d, ffn_hidden) def forward(self, x): x = x + self.attn(self.attn_norm(x)) # residual + attn x = x + self.ffn(self.ffn_norm(x)) # residual + FFN return x
What interviewers probe
Pre-norm vs post-normPre-norm (Llama, GPT-NeoX) trains stably at scale — gradient highway is the unnormalised residual stream. Post-norm (original 2017) needs warm-up + tuning.
ResidualsWithout them, gradient must traverse every sub-layer. With them, ∂loss/∂x = ∂loss/∂y · (1 + ∂F/∂x) — the +1 is the highway.
RMSNorm vs LayerNormRMSNorm drops the mean-subtraction; ~10% faster, same quality. Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek all use RMSNorm.
SwiGLU FFN3 matrices (gate, up, down). Gate · up creates a learned filter; outperforms plain GELU on every modern benchmark. Hidden dim usually 8/3 × d_model.
StackingStack N=32 (Llama 7B) to N=126 (DeepSeek 671B). Final RMSNorm + LM head → vocab logits. That’s the entire decoder LM.
Live coding gotcha: remember to transpose(1,2) before AND after attention so heads sit on dim 1 during the dot product but dim 2 when concatenating back. Off-by-one on this is the #1 failure mode in interviews.
Q&A Volume 1: Q1–Q60 (Foundations & Core) Click any question to expand. ML ENG AI ENG DATA SCI MLOps ARCHITECT ALL
How to Use This Volume
What is this volume? This is the foundations and core interview drill set, covering the first block of questions that show up across ML, LLM, systems, and architecture interviews.
Why it matters: These questions test whether your basics are fluent enough that the rest of the interview can go deeper instead of getting stuck on first principles.
How to use it: Expand one question at a time, answer aloud first, then compare your answer against the reference and jump back to the linked tab when you need to repair a gap.
Example: If you miss a question on LoRA or HNSW, revisit the Fine-tuning or RAG tab immediately, then return and answer the question again from memory.
Interview Loop
Pick 10 questions
→
Answer aloud first
→
Compare with reference
→
Revisit weak tab
→
Re-answer from memory
Pass Focus Action
1 Core recall Answer 10 questions without expanding them first.
2 Gap repair Use the linked source tab to repair weak concepts immediately.
3 Retention Re-answer the same set later from memory only.
Q1. Explain the transformer architecture from tokens to output probabilities.ALL
Token IDs embedding lookup (vocab_size d_model). Positional encoding: RoPE rotates Q,K vectors by angle ᵢpos where ᵢ=10000^(-2i/d). Encodes relative position in QK naturally. N transformer blocks: Pre-RMSNorm Multi-head causal self-attention (GQA in modern LLMs) residual add Pre-RMSNorm SwiGLU FFN (3 weight matrices: gateup then down projection) residual add. Output: Final RMSNorm linear to vocab_size logits temperature scaling sampling. KV cache: K,V from past tokens cached; only compute Q for new token at each generation step.
Q2. What is the vanishing gradient problem and how does modern deep learning solve it?ML ENG
Problem: In deep networks, backprop multiplies gradients through each layer: L/W = L/z z/z ... z/W. If each term < 1 (sigmoid/tanh saturate with max gradient 0.25): after 50 layers, gradient 0.25 0. First layers learn nothing.

Modern solutions:
  • Residual connections (ResNet/Transformers): y = F(x) + x. Gradient: L/x = L/y(F/x + 1). The +1 ensures gradient 1 regardless of F gradient highway even if sublayer contributes nothing.
  • ReLU activations: Gradient = 1 when positive no saturation. No multiplicative shrinkage.
  • Pre-norm (LayerNorm before sublayer): Controls activation scale, prevents both vanishing and exploding gradients.
  • AdamW: Adaptive per-parameter LR. Low gradients get amplified. Even small gradient signal is utilized.
Q3. Derive the gradient of softmax + cross-entropy loss.ML ENG
This is the most important gradient in deep learning. Given logits z, class labels y (one-hot), and softmax p_k = exp(z_k)/exp(z_j):

Loss: L = -_k y_k log(p_k)

Derivation: L/z_k = _j (-y_j/p_j) p_j/z_k

Using softmax derivatives: p_k/z_k = p_k(1-p_k) and p_j/z_k = -p_jp_k for jk:

L/z_k = -(y_k(1-p_k)) + _{jk} y_jp_k = -y_k + p_k_j y_j = p_k - y_k

The remarkable result: gradient is simply predicted probability minus true label. No saturation, always nonzero when prediction is wrong. This clean form is why softmax+CE is the universal classification loss.
Q4. What is LoRA and why does it work?ML ENG
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): Instead of updating W (dk parameters), learn W = BA where BR^(dr) and AR^(rk) with rmin(d,k). Forward pass: h = Wx + (BA/scaling)x.

Why it works: The change in weights needed for a new task lies in a low-dimensional subspace of the full weight space. Pre-trained model has rich representations task adaptation doesn't need all dk dimensions, just the r critical ones.

Parameter savings: r=16, d=k=4096: LoRA = 131K vs 16.7M (0.78%). Memory: only adapters need gradients base model in 4-bit NF4 (QLoRA).

Initialization: B=zeros, A=random Gaussian. So W=0 at start preserves pre-trained behavior exactly. Training gradually builds up the needed adaptation.

Merging: W_merged = W + (/r)BA. Zero inference overhead. Or keep separate for multi-task switching.
Q5. Explain RLHF step by step including the KL penalty.ALL
Step 1 SFT: Fine-tune base LLM on (prompt, ideal_response) pairs. Standard cross-entropy on completion tokens. Teaches format and basic instruction following.

Step 2 Reward Model: Collect human preference data: (prompt, response_A, response_B, preference). Train RM with loss: -log((r_winner - r_loser)). RM outputs scalar reward for any (prompt, response).

Step 3 PPO RL: Total reward = RM(x,y) - KL(_(y|x) _ref(y|x)). PPO optimizes this reward while clipping probability ratio to prevent large updates: min(r_tA_t, clip(r_t,1-,1+)A_t).

Why KL penalty: Without it, policy finds outputs that fool the reward model (reward hacking). Sycophancy, extreme verbosity, repetition of high-scoring phrases. KL term pulls policy toward SFT reference the known-good baseline. controls tradeoff: low = more RL gain but more hacking risk.
Q6. What is DPO and how does it differ from RLHF?ML ENG
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Eliminates reward model + RL training loop entirely. Uses the LLM itself as an implicit reward model.

Loss: L_DPO = -E[log ((log _(y_w|x)/_ref(y_w|x) - log _(y_l|x)/_ref(y_l|x)))]. y_w = preferred, y_l = rejected, _ref = frozen SFT.

Intuition: Push model to increase likelihood of preferred response (relative to reference) and decrease likelihood of rejected. The term prevents deviation from reference (like KL in RLHF).

Advantages over RLHF: No separate reward model (saves training + memory). No online sampling + PPO loop (simpler). More stable training. Equal or better alignment quality on most tasks.

When RLHF still preferred: Very precise multi-dimensional reward shaping. Large-scale with massive compute budget. Process reward model (verify reasoning steps, not just final answer, as in DeepSeek-R1/o1).
Q7. How does RAG differ from fine-tuning for knowledge injection?ALL
RAG: Retrieve relevant documents at query time, inject into context. Best for: large knowledge bases, frequently updated information, need for citations, proprietary enterprise data. Knowledge stays external always current. Can cite sources. Works immediately without training.

Fine-tuning: Bake knowledge into model weights via training. Best for: stable knowledge (writing style, format, domain behavior), reducing prompt complexity, task-specific capabilities. NOT for: specific facts (LLMs hallucinate fine-tuned facts, may extrapolate incorrectly).

The key mistake: Using fine-tuning to inject factual knowledge. 'Tell the model about our product catalog' via fine-tuning model hallucinates plausible product details with confidence. Always use RAG for facts, fine-tuning for behavior/style.

Hybrid (best for most production): RAG for knowledge + fine-tuning for response format, domain tone, and task structure. Neither alone handles all requirements.
Q8. Explain the HNSW algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search.AI ENG
HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World): Graph-based ANN index. Brute-force kNN is O(Nd) per query too slow for 10M+ vectors. HNSW achieves O(log N) with 95%+ recall.

Structure: Multi-layer graph. Top layers: few nodes with long-range connections (highway graph). Bottom layer: all nodes with short-range connections (local neighborhood). Exponentially fewer nodes per higher layer.

Search: Enter at random node at top layer greedily navigate to nearest neighbor descend to next layer repeat find top-k at base layer. Each layer refines the search in a smaller neighborhood.

Key params: M=16 (connections per node, more = better recall, more RAM). ef_construction=200 (candidates explored during build). ef_search=50 (candidates at query time, tune recall vs latency).

Recall vs latency: ef_search=10: ~88% recall, fastest. ef_search=200: ~99.5%, 10 slower. For RAG: 95% is usually sufficient and avoids unnecessary latency.
Q9. What is the difference between precision and recall and when do you optimize for each?DATA SCI
Precision = TP/(TP+FP): Of all predicted positives, what fraction are truly positive? Measures trustworthiness of positive predictions. Optimize when false positives are costly.

Recall = TP/(TP+FN): Of all actual positives, what fraction did you find? Measures coverage. Optimize when false negatives are costly.

Examples:
  • Spam filter: Optimize precision. Missing spam = annoying. Flagging legitimate email as spam = unacceptable.
  • Cancer screening: Optimize recall. Missing cancer = potentially fatal. False positive = further tests but manageable.
  • Legal document discovery: Optimize recall. Missing relevant document = legal risk. Reviewing extra docs = just work.
PR-AUC vs ROC-AUC: For imbalanced data (rare positive class), PR-AUC is more informative. ROC-AUC can be misleadingly high when negatives vastly outnumber positives (model looks good even if precision is terrible).
Q10. How do you handle class imbalance in a machine learning problem?DATA SCI
First: understand the imbalance. 1% positive rate vs 0.01% positive rate require different approaches. Identify: is the minority class truly rare or just undersampled?

Data-level: SMOTE (synthetic oversampling interpolate between existing minority points). Random undersampling (fast, may lose information). SMOTE+Tomek: oversample then clean noisy boundary samples. Apply only to training set.

Algorithm-level: Class weights: weight_pos = n_total/(n_classes n_pos). Pass to loss or estimator. class_weight='balanced' in sklearn. Focal loss: (1-p_t)^ CE, =2 standard. Downweights easy examples, focuses on hard ones.

Threshold tuning: Never use default 0.5 threshold on imbalanced data. Use precision-recall curve to find threshold that maximizes F1 or meets your FP/FN cost ratio.

Evaluation: Never report accuracy. Use F1, PR-AUC, Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Test set: keep imbalanced (don't balance it that's not the real distribution).
Q11. What is gradient clipping and when do you use it?ML ENG
Gradient clipping: If g > threshold: g = g (threshold/g). Preserves gradient direction. Clip by norm (standard) vs clip by value (changes direction, less common).

When mandatory: RNNs/LSTMs: BPTT multiplies gradients across T timesteps explodes exponentially for long sequences. LLM pre-training: occasional loss spikes due to bad batches. Without clipping, one bad batch corrupts training. All modern LLM training: GPT-4, LLaMA, Claude all use clip=1.0.

Typical values: max_grad_norm=1.0 for LLMs. 0.5 for RNNs. max_grad_norm applied after backward(), before optimizer.step().

Diagnosis: If you see NaN loss or weight norms exploding: gradient clipping is the fix. Monitor gradient norms during training sudden spike precedes explosion.

PyTorch: torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0). Call this every training step.
Q12. Explain the transformer pre-training objective and why it works so well.ALL
Causal language modeling (CLM): Given tokens x...x, predict x. Loss = -1/T log P(x|x...x). Applied to ALL positions simultaneously during training (teacher forcing). Simple but extraordinarily powerful at scale.

Why it works: To predict the next word accurately, the model MUST learn: grammar (syntax), facts (semantics), reasoning (can't predict conclusion without understanding argument), style (predict next word given established tone). All these learned implicitly as a byproduct of next-token prediction.

Why scaling helps: More parameters = more capacity to store world knowledge and patterns. More data = more facts, more reasoning patterns. More compute = more gradient updates. Smooth power-law improvement: loss scales predictably with compute/params/data.

Perplexity: exp(-1/N log P(xᵢ)) the effective vocabulary size the model is choosing from at each step. Lower = better. Random token predictor: perplexity = vocab_size. Human: ~40-80. GPT-4: ~6-8 on standard benchmarks.
Q13. How does speculative decoding work?ML ENG
Problem: Large models (70B+) generate one token at a time: each step requires full forward pass. Sequential = slow.

Speculative decoding: Small draft model (7B) generates K candidate tokens. Large target model verifies ALL K simultaneously in ONE forward pass. Accept/reject at each position based on probability ratio.

Acceptance rule: Token x accepted if: random_sample < P_target(x)/P_draft(x). If rejected: sample from corrected distribution. Return all accepted tokens + first rejected position's sample.

Why it works: Verification is a forward pass over K tokens (parallel) same cost as generating 1 token from scratch with the large model. If draft accuracy > 70%: accept ~3-5 tokens per target-model call 2-3 throughput.

Production use: vLLM with --speculative-model option. Best for: models where same-family smaller version exists (LLaMA 70B + LLaMA 7B draft). N-gram speculation: use n-gram model as draft, free (no extra model). Self-speculative: early exit layers as draft.
Q14. What are the key differences between BERT and GPT architectures?ML ENG
BERT (Encoder-only, bidirectional): Every token attends to ALL other tokens. Pre-training: MLM (predict masked tokens) + NSP. Best for: text understanding classification, NER, extractive QA, semantic similarity. Cannot generate text. Training is more data-efficient (dense prediction signal: every masked token).

GPT (Decoder-only, causal): Each token only attends to PREVIOUS tokens. Pre-training: next-token prediction. Best for: text generation, instruction following, reasoning, code. Also works for classification (via prompting).

Why decoder-only dominates now: Scales better (smoother loss curves with more compute/data). Emergent in-context learning at scale (BERT doesn't generalize the same way). Generation capability opens broader use cases. Simpler training objective scales predictably with Chinchilla laws.

Practical use: Still use BERT-family models for: embedding models (sentence-transformers), NER, classification where speed matters, cross-encoder reranking. Use GPT-family for everything generation-related.
Q15. How does Flash Attention work and why is it critical for long contexts?ML ENG
Standard attention problem: For T tokens, must materialize TT attention matrix in HBM (high-bandwidth GPU memory). At T=32K: 32K2 bytes = 2GB. Must read/write to HBM repeatedly. Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not compute.

Flash Attention solution: Compute attention in tiles that fit in SRAM (L1/L2 cache, ~50MB, 10-50 TB/s bandwidth). Key insight: use online softmax (Milakov & Gimelshein 2018) to compute running softmax normalization while processing tiles, without seeing all scores first.

Algorithm: Split Q into blocks. For each Q block: iterate over K,V blocks, compute partial attention and running softmax stats. Combine statistics to get exact softmax normalization. Never store full TT matrix.

Results: Same mathematical output as standard attention. O(T) memory vs O(T). 2-4 wall-clock speedup (IO-bound compute-bound). Required for 32K+ context on any GPU. Flash Attention 3 (H100): FP8, 1.5-2 over FA2.
Q16. Explain how you would evaluate a RAG system.AI ENG
Multi-layer evaluation strategy:

Layer 1 Retrieval quality: Context precision@k (fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant). Context recall (did we get all necessary chunks?). Run on test set of (query, relevant_docs) pairs. Tool: RAGAS with labeled ground truth.

Layer 2 Generation quality: Faithfulness (is every claim in the answer supported by retrieved context? LLM judge). Answer relevancy (does answer address the question?). Factual correctness vs ground truth answers. Tools: RAGAS, LangSmith, Langfuse.

Layer 3 End-to-end: Task completion rate (did user get what they needed?). Follow-up question rate (proxy for answer quality). User satisfaction (thumbs up/down). A/B test vs baseline (GPT-4 without RAG? Previous version?).

Layer 4 Failure analysis: No-context queries (does model correctly say 'I don't know'?). Multi-hop (requires combining multiple chunks). Contradictory documents. Stale content. Build test cases for each failure mode.

Production monitoring: Sample 5% of real traffic. Run faithfulness check. Alert if average faithfulness drops below threshold.
Q17. What is MoE (Mixture of Experts) and how is it trained?ML ENG
MoE replaces the dense FFN layer: Instead of one FFN (all params active every token), use N expert FFNs + a router. Router selects top-k experts per token (k=2 standard). Only k of N experts activated. Total params: N more. Active params: k (sparse computation).

Router: scores = Softmax(W_rx). Select top-k. Output = ᵢ score_i FFN_i(x). Weight output by router score.
