"Tell me about a technically difficult ML problem you solved." Your best war story model that wasn't working, found root cause, fixed it, quantified improvement.
Disagreement/conflict
"Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision." Show you can push back respectfully, use data to argue your case, and commit to the team decision when outvoted.
Failure/learning
"Tell me about a time your model/system failed in production." Shows maturity. Focus on: how you diagnosed, what you fixed, what process you put in place to prevent recurrence.
Leadership/influence
"Tell me about a time you led a technical initiative without formal authority." Drove adoption of new framework, established ML standards, mentored someone.
Ambiguity/ownership
"Tell me about a time you had to figure out the right problem to solve." You identified a problem nobody else had defined, proposed approach, got buy-in, executed.
Portfolio Presentation for AI Engineers
💻 GitHub Profile
  • Pinned repos: 4-6 best projects with clear READMEs
  • README must include: problem, approach, results (with numbers), how to run
  • At least 1 project with: training code + evaluation + serving
  • Activity graph: consistent commits show active learning
  • Contributions to known repos (HuggingFace, LangChain) = credibility multiplier
📋 Project Talking Points
  • Problem: Why this problem? What was the business/technical gap?
  • Approach: What did you try first? What didn't work and why?
  • Results: Concrete numbers. % improvement. Cost saved. Users served.
  • Trade-offs: What would you do differently? What are the limitations?
  • Scale: What breaks at 10 load? How would you fix it?
💰 Salary Negotiation
  • Never give a number first "What is the budgeted range for this role?"
  • Research: levels.fyi for comp data at specific companies/levels
  • Negotiate everything: base, equity (cliff, vesting), signing, PTO
  • Counter with data: "Based on levels.fyi, IC5 ML at [company] is $XY"
  • Get 2+ competing offers before negotiating increases leverage 4060%
  • Always negotiate in writing after verbal offer gives you time to think
Common Interview Mistakes to Avoid
MistakeWhy It HurtsWhat to Do Instead
Jumping to code without clarifyingSignals junior thinking solving the wrong problem elegantlyAlways spend 2-3 min clarifying scope, constraints, success criteria
Only knowing the happy pathProduction engineers must think about what breaksProactively mention: "What if the model degrades?" "What if the data has nulls?"
"We did X" instead of "I did X"Interviewers can't assess your individual contributionOwn your work: "I designed the feature store schema. My teammate handled the serving layer."
Memorising answers without understandingInterviewers probe one level deeper you get exposed immediatelyUnderstand the WHY. If you can't explain it to a 10-year-old, you don't understand it yet.
No questions for the interviewerSignals lack of curiosity or preparationAlways have 2-3 genuine questions ready about the team's challenges
Over-engineering the answerSignals you can't distinguish important from unimportant complexity"The simplest solution that works is X. I'd only add Y if we hit Z scale threshold."
Underselling impact with vague language"Improved performance" means nothing every project "improved" somethingAlways quantify: "Reduced p99 latency from 2.3s to 0.4s, enabling 3 new customer segments"
AI Career Path understanding the progression from junior to staff/principal engineer, what actually gets you promoted, how to build a personal brand in AI, and how to break into the field from other backgrounds. The skills in this document get you interviews. This tab helps you navigate the career.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
IC Levels What Each Level Actually Means in AI
IC3 Junior / New Grad ML Engineer
Scope: Well-defined tasks, clear requirements, close mentorship. Output: Implements features, fixes bugs in existing systems, runs experiments with guidance. What they don't do: Define what to build, handle ambiguity, lead others. Typical comp (US FAANG): $200280K total.
IC4 Mid-level ML Engineer (most common level)
Scope: Owns features end-to-end. Works independently. Some ambiguity. Output: Designs and ships complete ML features. Onboards IC3s. Makes reasonable technical decisions independently. Growth unlock: Proactively identifies problems before being asked. Comp: $280400K.
IC5 Senior ML Engineer (the target for most experienced engineers)
Scope: Owns a domain (e.g., "the recommendation system"). Defines technical direction. Mentors IC3/4. Output: Technical design docs, cross-team influence, code reviews, drives standards. Growth unlock: Makes the team around you better, not just yourself. Comp: $380550K.
IC6 Staff ML Engineer (large scope, high ambiguity)
Scope: Owns a product area or platform. Sets technical direction across multiple teams. Deals with org-level ambiguity. Output: Architectural decisions with multi-year impact, hiring bar-raiser, technical strategy. Growth unlock: Can identify problems that don't exist yet. Comp: $500K900K+.
IC7+ Principal / Distinguished Engineer (rare, high impact)
Scope: Company-level technical problems. May work across the entire engineering org. External reputation (papers, talks, open source). Output: Defines the company's technical future. Comp: $800K$2M+ (mostly equity at this level).
What Actually Gets You Promoted
IC4 IC5 (most common promotion path)
What changes between IC4 and IC5?
IC4 thinks about
"How do I implement this feature correctly and on time?"
IC5 thinks about
"Is this the right feature to build? What are the downstream effects on the system? What technical debt does this create?"
Key promotable behaviours
Proactively identifies problems before asked. Writes design docs others use. Unblocks teammates. Gives feedback in code reviews that prevents future bugs, not just current ones.
Common mistake
Being the best IC4 (executes perfectly) but never showing IC5 behaviours (defines what to execute). Promotion requires demonstrating the NEXT level, not perfecting the current one.
Building Visibility (critical for promotion)
  • Write, don't just code: Design docs, postmortems, technical blog posts. Writing amplifies your impact beyond your immediate team.
  • Present at team meetings: "I tried X, it failed because Y, next time I'd do Z." Teaching colleagues is IC5 behaviour, not IC4.
  • Find the high-leverage work: Some projects have 10 the visibility. Choose projects that multiple teams depend on over projects only your team sees.
  • Have a sponsor (not just a mentor): A mentor gives advice. A sponsor advocates for you in rooms you're not in. Find someone at IC6+ who will speak for you at promotion committees.
  • Quantify everything: "I improved the model" vs "I reduced inference latency by 62%, enabling product to launch in 3 new markets." The second gets into your promo doc.
  • External credibility: One well-cited paper, one popular GitHub repo, one conference talk = you are mentioned in hiring conversations even when you're not looking.
Breaking Into AI from Other Backgrounds
BackgroundTransferable StrengthsGaps to FillBest Entry Path
Software Engineer (SWE)Strong coding, systems thinking, debugging, production experienceML theory (backprop, statistics), model evaluation, data pipelinesFast-track: build end-to-end ML project. Target MLOps roles first, then ML Engineer roles after 1 year.
Data AnalystStrong SQL, statistics, business metrics, data intuitionPython ML stack, model training and serving, software engineering practicesMove to Data Scientist first. Learn scikit-learn, PyTorch basics. Then move toward ML Engineer.
Academic ResearcherDeep theory, paper reading, experimental rigour, statisticsProduction engineering, distributed systems, cost/latency awareness, shipping fastStrong pipeline for research-heavy teams (Anthropic, DeepMind, MSR). Add MLOps project to show production ability.
Domain Expert (doctor, lawyer, finance)Deep domain knowledge, use case identification, evaluation judgmentTechnical ML skills, coding, data pipelinesAI Product Manager AI Product Lead. Or: domain-specific AI Engineer at vertical-focused startups (medical AI, legal AI).
DevOps / InfrastructureKubernetes, CI/CD, monitoring, cloud platforms, reliabilityML-specific operations (model drift, experiment tracking, feature stores)MLOps Engineer directly. Extremely in demand. Add MLflow/Feast knowledge to existing infra skills.
Building Your AI Brand Open Source & Community
💻 Open Source
  • Contribute to HuggingFace Transformers even documentation fixes count
  • Build a dataset on HuggingFace Hub 500+ downloads = credibility
  • Create a useful PEFT fine-tuning recipe people cite these
  • Publish benchmark results comparing models gets picked up by newsletters
  • Even 1 well-known repo beats 10 unknown ones
📝 Writing
  • One deep technical blog post per month beats daily shallow posts
  • Best topics: "I implemented X from scratch and learned Y"
  • Cross-post: personal blog + Medium + Substack
  • Share on X/LinkedIn with the code link algorithm rewards code posts
  • Guest post on Towards Data Science, The Gradient
🎬 Talks & Community
  • Start: local ML meetups. No slides needed for first talk.
  • Progress to: PyData, MLOPS World, applied ML conferences
  • ICLR/NeurIPS papers: 1 accepted paper = 5 years of credibility
  • Kaggle: top 100 in one competition = strong signal for ML Eng roles
  • Discord communities: Eleuther AI, LM Systems active participation noticed
2025 AI Job Market Reality
FAANG AI Headcount: A Mixed Picture
Meta, Google, Amazon ran significant ML team reductions 2023-2024 cutting roles with unclear product impact. Meanwhile applied AI teams (LLM products, RAG, agents) are actively hiring. The divide: product-impact AI roles expand; pure-research roles at non-frontier labs contract. Know which world you are interviewing for.
Where AI Roles Are Concentrated (2025)
Tier 1 highest demand: LLM/AI Engineer (RAG, agents, fine-tuning pipelines), AI Platform/MLOps. Tier 2: ML Engineer (classical + LLM), Data Scientist with causal inference expertise. Tier 3 most competitive: Pure ML Research Engineer outside OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta FAIR.
Compensation Has Bifurcated Sharply
Frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI): $400K-$2M+ driven by pre-IPO equity. Large tech (FAANG+): $280-$550K IC4-IC6. Enterprise AI: $180-$320K. Leverage is highest with 2+ competing offers, especially one from a frontier lab. Never negotiate without competing data.
What Employers Actually Want in 2025
Ship speed over research depth. Production LLM experience beats 10 papers. Evaluation competence (RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, golden datasets) is a differentiator most engineers cannot build eval pipelines. Cost consciousness: "what does this cost at 10M requests/day?" is now a standard interview question. OWASP LLM security awareness tested at architect level.
IC Track vs Management Track
IC Track (Individual Contributor to Staff+)
  • IC5 to IC6 (Staff): Define technical direction across multiple teams. Architectural decisions with multi-year impact. Write design docs that shape the platform for years.
  • IC7 (Principal): Company-level technical strategy. External credibility via papers and talks. Often spans the entire engineering organisation.
  • Best for: People energised by deep technical problems. Don't want 60% of time in 1:1s and performance reviews.
  • Comp ceiling: IC7+ at FAANG can exceed $2M equity-heavy. Staff engineers at AI startups pre-IPO can exceed this.
  • Hardest gate: IC5 to IC6 requires demonstrated cross-team impact and sponsorship from a VP-level engineer or above.
Management Track (EM to Director)
  • Eng Manager to Senior EM to Director: Your metric shifts from personal output to team throughput. Responsible for hiring, performance, roadmap delivery.
  • Best for: People energised by growing others. Prefer influence through people over influence through code.
  • The trap: Many strong ICs become weak EMs. Coding skills atrophy, technical credibility erodes, and the work does not energise them. Never switch for comp alone.
  • In AI specifically: Technical EM is highly valued an EM who can still read code, review architectures, and hold engineers accountable technically. Far rarer than non-technical management.
  • When to switch: If you are consistently doing unasked-for management work (mentoring, coordinating, unblocking) and finding it energising that is the signal.
How to Evaluate an AI Team Before Joining
Ask these in your interviews the answers reveal engineering culture
How do you evaluate your AI features?
Red flag: "We look at user feedback." Green flag: "Golden datasets per feature, RAGAS scores in CI, deploy blocked on regression." Evaluation maturity is the single strongest signal of AI engineering culture.
What is your model deployment process?
Red flag: "We push to prod and watch it." Green flag: "Shadow then canary then full rollout with automated rollback on faithfulness drop." Shows production risk discipline.
How do you handle prompt changes?
Red flag: "Update in code and redeploy." Green flag: "Versioned artifact registry, playground testing against golden set, A/B before production." Shows engineering discipline around non-deterministic systems.
What is your biggest AI production failure?
Red flag: Can't name one (too new, or dishonest). Green flag: Specific story with diagnosis, fix, and process change. Teams that share failures learn; teams that hide them repeat them.
How do you decide what to build with AI vs without?
Red flag: "We add AI wherever we can." Green flag: "We use a framework: RAG for knowledge, agents for multi-step, fine-tune for format/style, otherwise prompting or no AI." Shows they resist hype.
What does Day 1 look like for an AI engineer?
Red flag: "Here is the repo, good luck." Green flag: "Sandbox access, starter templates, cookbooks, assigned buddy for four weeks." Shows investment in developer experience and onboarding.
Emerging Trends in AI
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro — done at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going for architect-level detail.
What Is It (Plain English)
Emerging trends in AI are the capability shifts happening right now that will change what you can build, what it costs, and how it should be governed — within the next 12–24 months.
As of mid-2026, five shifts are material enough that architects designing systems today need to account for them:
  • Reasoning models: A new class of models that "think before they answer," dramatically improving accuracy on complex multi-step problems at the cost of higher latency and token usage
  • Mixture of Experts (MoE): The architecture behind every frontier model — explains why large models can be cost-competitive despite their apparent size
  • Computer Use / GUI agents: Models that can operate software interfaces, opening automation use cases that previously required RPA or custom integration
  • Persistent memory agents: Agents that maintain context across sessions, enabling genuinely stateful AI assistants
  • Multimodal-first models: The frontier is no longer text-only; image, audio, and video input/output is standard, changing document intake and media processing architectures
This tab is a radar, not a textbook. The goal is to have enough understanding to know which trends affect your current or near-term projects, and when to revisit the specialist tabs for deeper coverage.
Why Should I Care
For Solution Architects

Emerging trends affect system design decisions you make today:

  • Reasoning models change when to use expensive frontier models vs cheap models. A task that previously required GPT-4o may now be better served by o3-mini or DeepSeek-R1. The capability tier map needs quarterly recalibration.
  • MoE architecture explains why model size no longer linearly maps to inference cost. A "400B parameter" MoE model activates only ~40B parameters per token — understanding this prevents you from dismissing large models as automatically too expensive.
  • Computer Use opens automation of legacy workflows that have no API: reading a CRM screen, filling in a web form, navigating an internal portal. For integration architects dealing with legacy system estates, this is a significant unlock.
  • Persistent memory changes the architecture of AI assistants. A stateless API call is sufficient for a one-off task; a persistent-memory agent changes how you design conversation history, user context, and personalisation.
For Enterprise Architects

Trends that create new governance requirements:

  • Agentic AI with computer use can take actions in systems not designed for automation — creating audit trail gaps, bypassing approval workflows, accessing data without conventional access controls. Governance frameworks designed for "AI that answers questions" are insufficient for "AI that operates software."
  • Reasoning model token usage (thinking tokens) can be 5–20× the output token count, dramatically changing cost projections for use cases that adopt reasoning models.
  • Persistent memory means AI systems now have state that persists across sessions — creating data residency, retention, and privacy questions that stateless models do not raise.
Think About It Like This
The Skilled Trades Analogy

Traditional AI (pre-reasoning models) is like hiring a fast typist: give them a clear task, they execute it immediately. Great at well-defined, single-step tasks. They struggle with problems that require stopping to think, consulting references, and checking work.

Reasoning models are like hiring a junior consultant taught to "think out loud before answering." Before responding, they write a scratchpad: "The question is X. The key constraint is Y. Let me check if approach A satisfies Y... it does partially, but fails on Z. Let me try approach B..." This thinking process is invisible to the client but produces dramatically better final answers on hard problems — at the cost of a longer meeting.

Mixture of Experts is like a law firm with 100 specialist partners, but only 8–10 work on any given client matter. The firm is large (lots of expertise available), but efficient (only the relevant specialists are activated for each task). The per-matter cost is similar to a smaller generalist firm — but quality on specialised matters is much higher.

Computer Use agents are like giving an intern a computer and saying "log in to the supplier portal and download all invoices from last month." You don't write an API integration — you explain the task and they navigate the interface the same way a human would. Powerful for legacy systems; risks the same mistakes an unsupervised intern might make.

Persistent memory is the difference between a new temp who starts fresh every day and a long-tenured colleague who remembers your preferences, past decisions, and project history. The long-tenured colleague is far more useful — but you also need to think carefully about what they "remember" and whether that memory is accurate.
Step-by-Step Walkthrough — The Core Concepts
4.1 Reasoning Models: Thinking Before Answering
What they are
Reasoning models (OpenAI o1, o3, o4-mini; DeepSeek-R1; Qwen QwQ; Gemini Thinking) are LLMs trained to generate an internal "chain of thought" scratchpad before producing their final answer. The scratchpad — called "thinking tokens" — is typically hidden from the user but consumes tokens (and therefore costs money and time).
Why this matters
Standard LLMs generate responses one token at a time, left-to-right, without backtracking. If the model commits to an incorrect direction early, it continues in that direction. Reasoning models effectively implement a "draft, evaluate, revise" loop at training time — the model learns to explore multiple approaches before committing to an answer.
Performance uplift
On tasks requiring multi-step logic (mathematics, code that must pass tests, legal reasoning, complex planning), reasoning models typically outperform standard models by 20–40% on accuracy benchmarks. The gain is smaller on well-specified, single-step tasks (classification, summarisation, format conversion) — for those, a standard model is usually sufficient and cheaper.
The cost / latency trade-off
Standard model (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4o): Thinking tokens: 0 Time to response: 1–5 seconds Cost: £1–5 / M tokens Reasoning model (o3, DeepSeek-R1): Thinking tokens: 1,000–20,000 per response (hidden but billed) Time to response: 10–60 seconds (variable — depends on problem difficulty) Cost: £5–30 / M tokens (thinking + output)
When to use reasoning models
Use caseReasoning model?Why
Complex code generation (must pass tests)✓ YesMulti-step logic; quality outweighs cost
Mathematical / quantitative analysis✓ YesReasoning errors are expensive
Legal document analysis with complex dependencies✓ YesHigh-stakes; accuracy critical
Customer service Q&A✗ NoSingle-step; speed matters; cost not justified
Product description generation✗ NoCreative, single-pass; standard model fine
Data extraction from documents✗ NoFormat + instruction following, not logic
Architecture / system design planning✓ YesComplex trade-off analysis benefits from thinking

The architect's rule: use reasoning models when (a) the task requires multi-step logic and (b) the cost of a wrong answer exceeds the cost of the thinking tokens. For high-volume, latency-sensitive tasks: standard models. For low-volume, high-stakes, complex tasks: reasoning models.

4.2 Mixture of Experts (MoE): Why Frontier Models Stay Affordable
The problem MoE solves
A dense model with 400B parameters activates all 400B parameters for every input token. This is computationally expensive — cost scales with parameter count.
How MoE works
Instead of a single dense feed-forward network, a MoE model has N "expert" networks (typically 64–256 per layer) and a learned "router" that selects K of them (typically 2–8) for each token. Only the selected experts are activated — the rest are idle.
Dense 400B model: Every token → ALL 400B parameters activated MoE 400B model (e.g. 64 experts, top-2 routing): Every token → router selects 2 of 64 experts Active parameters per token: 400B × (2/64) ≈ 12.5B Inference cost ≈ equivalent to a 12.5B dense model But model quality ≈ a 400B+ dense model (all experts available during training)
Why MoE matters for architects
  • Quality vs cost decoupling: MoE models can be larger (and better quality) than their inference cost implies. A "400B parameter MoE model" does not cost 4× what a "100B dense model" costs to serve.
  • Prevalence: Every major frontier model (GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, Llama-3-405B) is widely believed to use MoE architecture.
  • Reliability risk: Router failures (load imbalance — too many tokens routed to the same experts) are the primary reliability risk. MoE models need load-balancing losses during training to prevent expert collapse.

For architects: "Parameter count" is not a reliable proxy for "inference cost" in 2026. A 400B MoE model may be cheaper to serve than a 70B dense model for the same quality level. Always benchmark on your task before making infrastructure decisions based on parameter count alone.

4.3 Computer Use / GUI Agents: Automation Without APIs
What it is
Computer Use is the capability of a vision-language model to observe a screenshot of a computer interface and generate mouse/keyboard actions to accomplish a goal — navigating through a GUI the same way a human would.

Anthropic's Computer Use, available via API, takes input: a screenshot + a task description. Output: a series of mouse clicks, keyboard inputs, and scroll actions that accomplish the task.
Why this matters for enterprise architects
Most enterprise systems were built long before they were expected to be automated. Computer Use opens automation for:
  • Legacy ERP and CRM: SAP GUI, legacy Oracle screens without API integration
  • Supplier portals: No EDI/API support
  • Government / regulatory portals: Reporting platforms with GUI-only access
  • Internal legacy tools: Built before API-first design was standard
The architecture pattern
Task: "Download all invoices from supplier portal for October 2026" 1. Agent receives task + initial screenshot of portal login page 2. Vision model identifies: username field, password field, login button 3. Actions: click username → type credentials → click password → type → click login 4. New screenshot: dashboard 5. Vision model identifies: "Invoices" menu item 6. Actions: click Invoices → set date filter → click Download 7. Screenshot confirms: download started 8. Task complete; file in local filesystem
Governance risks (non-trivial)
  • Unintended actions: The model may click the wrong button or take an action in a system that cannot be undone. Computer Use agents need sandboxed environments and human review for consequential actions.
  • Audit trail: Traditional RPA leaves a detailed audit log. Computer Use agents require explicit logging infrastructure — screenshots and action sequences must be captured for compliance.
  • Access control: The agent operates with the credentials of whoever is logged in. Over-privileged accounts can access more than the task requires.
  • Error recovery: When the agent gets stuck (unexpected page state, CAPTCHA, error message), it needs explicit fallback logic — unlike a human, it may silently fail or loop indefinitely.
When to use Computer Use vs traditional integration
ScenarioComputer UseAPI / RPA
No API available, no budget to build one✓✗
Action is high-frequency (>1K/day)✗ (fragile at scale)✓
Action requires human judgement on screen✓✗
Regulatory audit trail required✗ (needs careful logging)✓
Proof of concept / validation✓✗
4.4 Persistent Memory Agents: Stateful AI Assistants
The stateless problem
Every standard LLM API call is stateless. The model has no memory of previous calls. For a customer service agent, this means the customer must re-explain their context every session. For an internal AI assistant, the model can't remember that "you prefer concise summaries" or "the project uses AWS, not Azure."
The MemGPT / Letta pattern
Persistent memory architectures maintain a structured memory store outside the context window, with explicit read/write operations:
User: "What was the conclusion of yesterday's supplier meeting?" Agent: 1. Search memory store: query("supplier meeting", date=yesterday) 2. Retrieve: [Meeting notes: Supplier X agreed to 10% price reduction for Q4] 3. Include in context → generate response 4. After response: update memory if new information emerged
Memory taxonomy (four types)
  • Working memory: Current context window — ephemeral, lost after session
  • Episodic memory: Past interactions and events — retrieved by similarity/recency
  • Semantic memory: General knowledge and user preferences — structured facts
  • Procedural memory: Learned workflows and skills — how to do specific tasks
Enterprise architecture implications
Persistent memory creates new data assets that need governance:
  • PII in memory stores: Memory may contain user data subject to data protection regulation
  • Retention policies: Memory retention must align with data governance — how long do you store AI conversation history?
  • Memory accuracy degradation: The AI's memory of "the project uses AWS" becomes wrong when you migrate to Azure — there must be a mechanism to correct and expire stale memories
  • Access control: Multi-user memory systems must enforce ACL — one user's memories must not leak to another
4.5 Multimodal-First Architecture: 2026 Standard
What changed
Capability20232026
Image inputGPT-4V only (expensive)All frontier models; mid-tier too
Document (PDF) inputRequires OCR preprocessingNative in-context PDF reading
Audio inputWhisper transcription then LLMEnd-to-end (GPT-4o, Gemini)
Video inputNot availableGemini 1.5 Pro, some others
Image generationSeparate model (DALL-E 3)Integrated in some (GPT-4o)
System design patterns that change
Old pattern for document intake:
PDF → OCR (Tesseract/AWS Textract) → text → LLM
2026 pattern:
PDF → multimodal LLM (reads layout + text natively)
The old pattern requires an OCR pipeline, error handling for OCR failures, and loses layout information (tables, columns, headings that indicate structure). The 2026 pattern handles the full document natively — at API pricing, often cheaper than maintaining a separate OCR pipeline.
When to still use dedicated OCR
Very high volume (1M+ documents/month where per-document API cost matters), documents requiring 100% field extraction accuracy with structured validation, or regulatory requirements for a specific certified OCR system.
Enterprise Example
Upgrading a Procurement Automation System

A manufacturing company handles 2,000 supplier invoices/month and 500 purchase orders/day across 150 suppliers. Existing automation: EDI connections to 20 large suppliers (60% of volume); manual data entry for the remaining 40% (80 suppliers with no API/EDI); a 3-person team processing manual invoices at ~8 minutes each = 200 hours/month.

Problem 1: Manual invoice processing (40% of volume)

80 non-EDI suppliers send invoices via email as PDF attachments, images, or photos of handwritten invoices. Custom OCR pipelines exist for the 5 largest; the long tail is manual.

2026 solution: multimodal LLM invoice extraction

  • Send each attachment directly to Claude Vision
  • Prompt: extract structured fields (vendor name, invoice number, date, line items, totals, payment terms)
  • Structured output (JSON) feeds directly into the ERP
  • Accuracy on typed invoices: 97%. Handwritten: 88% — human review queue for low-confidence extractions
  • Result: 80% of previously manual invoices processed automatically; 200 → 40 hours/month manual work
Problem 2: 15 suppliers with no API and complex portal UIs

These suppliers require logging into individual supplier portals to download invoices — everything lives in a portal requiring navigation.

2026 solution: Computer Use agent for portal automation

  • Agent trained on each portal's navigation pattern
  • Scheduled nightly run: log in, navigate to invoices section, apply date filter, download
  • Files transferred to ERP intake folder
  • Governance: all screenshots captured to audit log; credentials stored in vault (not in agent prompt)
  • Result: 15 portals automated, covering ~200 invoices/month previously requiring dedicated human time
Problem 3: Purchase order exception handling

500 daily POs have an 8% exception rate (price mismatch, quantity discrepancy, terms issue). Each exception requires reading the PO, comparing to the supplier agreement, and deciding: approve, reject, or escalate.

2026 solution: reasoning model for exception analysis

  • Exception POs routed to reasoning model pipeline (o3-mini)
  • Context: PO details + relevant supplier agreement clause + approval history
  • Model produces: recommended action + reasoning trace
  • Human reviewer sees: recommendation + the model's reasoning chain
  • Result: 65% of exceptions processed automatically; manager time on exceptions reduced by 70%
Combined impact
Manual processing hours200 → 55/month
Portal downloading40 person-hours/month → near zero
Exception handling30 → 9 person-hours/month
Total saving~200 hours/month at £35/hr = £84,000/year
AI cost~£800/month (API + infrastructure)
ROI8.75× in year 1
Architecture Perspective
The Agentic Architecture Evolution
The most significant architectural shift is the move from reactive AI (model called, model responds, done) to agentic AI (model takes multi-step actions, maintains state, operates tools, potentially over extended time periods).
2023 AI system architecture: User input → LLM API call → response → user 2026 AI system architecture: User task → Agent runtime ├── Reasoning model (complex planning) ├── Tool calls (APIs, databases, computer use) ├── Memory read/write (persistent context) ├── Sub-agent delegation (specialist agents) └── Human-in-the-loop gates (for consequential actions) → Result delivered (possibly after minutes of work)
Durability Agents may run for minutes or hours — the system must handle interruptions gracefully (agent checkpointing, resume-on-failure).
Observability Multi-step agent traces are much more complex than single API calls — full trace logging with tool call sequences and reasoning steps is required, not optional.
Safety gates Consequential actions (sending an email, modifying a database, executing a financial transaction) need explicit human review gates — not assumed approval.
Cost control A misbehaving agent can generate thousands of API calls before being stopped — budget limits and circuit breakers are mandatory, not nice-to-have.
Framework Comparison for Agentic Systems
FrameworkBest forKey limitation
LangGraphStateful multi-step agents; production-ready; explicit state machineMore complex to set up than LangChain
LangChainRapid prototyping; broad tool ecosystemOpaque internals; hard to debug at scale
AutoGen (Microsoft)Multi-agent conversation patternsLess production-hardened than LangGraph
CrewAIRole-based multi-agent workflows; simple to configureLess flexible than LangGraph for complex state
Custom (direct SDK)Full control; maximum debuggabilityMaximum build effort
For production enterprise systems: LangGraph or direct SDK.
For prototyping and exploration: LangChain or CrewAI.
Check Yourself
Five Architect-Level Questions
  1. Reasoning model for all chatbot calls: A developer proposes using a reasoning model (o3) for all LLM calls in a customer service chatbot to "maximise quality." What are the problems with this approach?
    Expected: Three problems. (1) Cost: thinking tokens (1,000–20,000 per response) at £10–30/M tokens make high-volume use prohibitive for simple queries like "what are your opening hours?" (2) Latency: reasoning models take 10–60 seconds per response — unacceptable for a chatbot requiring 1–3 second response times. (3) Wasted capability: quality improvement from reasoning is concentrated on complex multi-step logic; simple FAQs and order status queries see minimal improvement over a standard model. Correct approach: route by complexity — standard model for simple queries, reasoning model for complex dispute resolution or multi-part technical support.
  2. Computer Use governance controls: Your organisation wants to automate downloading invoices from 20 supplier portals using Computer Use. What governance controls must be in place before this goes live?
    Expected: Minimum five. (1) Audit logging: every session must capture a complete screenshot trail and action sequence to an immutable store. (2) Credential management: portal credentials stored in a secrets vault — the agent retrieves credentials at runtime via API call; credentials are never exposed in logs or prompts. (3) Access scoping: minimum-privilege account for each portal — read-only where possible; review for unneeded write/admin permissions. (4) Human review gate: any action that modifies portal data requires explicit human approval. (5) Failure handling and alerting: when the agent gets stuck, it must alert a human rather than retrying indefinitely — define max retries, alert threshold, and fallback to manual process. Additionally: a scope statement limiting what the agent is authorised to do in each portal.
  3. MoE intuition: Explain Mixture of Experts at an intuition level. Why does it mean "400B parameters" doesn't mean "expensive to run"?
    Expected: In a dense model, every token processed activates every parameter. In a MoE model, each transformer layer contains multiple "expert" sub-networks and a learned router that selects only 2–8 experts per token. The other experts are idle. A 400B MoE model with 64 experts and top-2 routing activates approximately 400B × (2/64) ≈ 12.5B parameters per token — inference cost is closer to a 12–15B dense model, while model quality reflects having 400B parameters trained across all experts. For architects: always ask whether a model is dense or MoE, and what the active parameter count is, before making cost or infrastructure sizing decisions.
  4. Persistent memory data governance: A product team wants to build a persistent-memory AI assistant for their sales team that remembers customer preferences, past meeting notes, and deal history across sessions. What data governance questions must be resolved first?
    Expected: Five minimum. (1) Data classification: what category of data will be stored — customer PII, deal financials, competitive intelligence? Each may have different retention rules, access controls, and geographic restrictions. (2) Retention and deletion: how long is memory retained? What triggers deletion (GDPR request, employee leaving, account closed)? (3) Access control: can salesperson A see salesperson B's customer memories? Memory access must follow the same ACL as the underlying CRM data. (4) Memory accuracy and right to correction: who can correct stale or incorrect memories, and how? (5) Regulatory context: in financial services, customer interaction records may be subject to MiFID II, FCA rules, or similar — memory records may constitute "records of advice" with retention and disclosure requirements.
  5. Standard LLM vs reasoning model: What is the difference between a standard LLM and a reasoning model, and how does the "thinking tokens" mechanism improve accuracy on complex tasks?
    Expected: A standard LLM generates tokens left-to-right in a single pass — no mechanism to backtrack and reconsider an early decision. For multi-step reasoning, the model can go down the wrong path early and produce a plausible-sounding but incorrect result. A reasoning model generates a hidden "thinking" sequence before the final answer. During this thinking phase, the model explores multiple approaches, checks intermediate results for consistency, identifies when an approach isn't working, and backtracks. The final answer is generated only after the thinking process reaches a conclusion. Accuracy improvement comes from (a) more compute budget to explore the problem space before committing and (b) training objectives that reward correct final answers. Practically: 20–40% more accurate than standard models on complex logic benchmarks; marginal improvement on simple tasks where the cost/latency overhead is not justified.
Advanced Deep Dive
🟡 Optional depth — This section covers structured output (now the 2026 production standard) and agent observability tooling in depth. Safe to skip on a first pass.
8.1 Structured Output: The 2026 Production Standard
Why Structured Output Is Now Default

Structured output mode (JSON mode with schema enforcement) is now available from all major providers and should be the default for any integration use case. The mechanism: structured output constrains token sampling at the generation layer. The model can only generate tokens that result in valid JSON matching the provided schema — it is not just instructed to produce JSON, it is constrained to at the sampling level. This eliminates the entire category of "model produced invalid JSON" failures.

from pydantic import BaseModel from typing import Literal class InvoiceExtraction(BaseModel): vendor_name: str invoice_number: str total_amount: float currency: Literal["GBP", "EUR", "USD"] line_items: list[dict] confidence: Literal["high", "medium", "low"] # OpenAI structured output response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse( model="gpt-4o", messages=[...], response_format=InvoiceExtraction, ) invoice = response.choices[0].message.parsed # typed InvoiceExtraction object

The schema is transmitted to the model and constrains token generation. No JSON parsing errors, no defensive try/except, no format retry logic needed. For any new integration use case: start with structured output mode.

8.2 Agent Observability Tooling: LangSmith, Langfuse, and Arize
When agents run in production, traditional application monitoring is not enough. An agent that calls 12 tools across 4 reasoning steps generates a trace that looks nothing like a standard API call — you need tooling designed for the agent execution model.
ToolBest forKey capabilityCost model
LangSmith (LangChain)Teams using LangChain/LangGraphDeep integration with LangChain primitives; trace every agent stepPaid per trace volume
Langfuse (open-source)Teams wanting self-hosted observabilityFull trace capture, LLM-as-judge scoring, dataset managementFree self-hosted; cloud tier available
Arize PhoenixProduction monitoring at scaleDrift detection, hallucination scoring, embedding driftEnterprise pricing
What these tools capture
  • Full agent reasoning trace: Every step, tool call, and intermediate output
  • Token consumption per step: For cost attribution across use cases
  • Latency per step: To find the bottleneck in a multi-step agent
  • Output quality scores: Via LLM-as-judge or human review
  • Prompt version tracking: Which prompt version produced which output
For architects — centralise or federate? A single observability platform across all AI systems gives portfolio-level visibility (cost, quality, incidents). Per-team tooling gives flexibility but makes cross-system comparisons hard.

Minimum viable monitoring setup for a production agent: (1) full trace capture to a queryable store, (2) token cost attribution per use case, (3) weekly LLM-as-judge quality sampling on 1–5% of traces, (4) alert on latency or cost anomalies.

When to invest in this: As soon as your first agent goes to production. Debugging a production agent without trace capture is like debugging a distributed system without logs — technically possible, but extremely costly in engineer time.
Key Takeaways
  • Reasoning models are a specialised tool, not an upgrade for all LLM tasks. Use them when the task requires backtracking, checking intermediate results, or exploring multiple approaches — legal analysis, complex code, quantitative reasoning. For high-volume, latency-sensitive, or single-step tasks, standard models are faster and cheaper with negligible quality loss.
  • Mixture of Experts means parameter count is no longer a reliable proxy for inference cost. A 400B MoE model may be cheaper to serve than a 70B dense model at equivalent quality. Always ask whether a model uses MoE architecture and what the active parameter count is before making infrastructure sizing or cost decisions based on the headline parameter count.
  • Computer Use opens automation of legacy systems without APIs — but requires explicit governance controls before any enterprise deployment. Audit logging of all actions, credential vault management, minimum-privilege scoping, and human-in-the-loop gates for consequential actions are not optional. Computer Use without these controls creates audit trail gaps and access control risks that outweigh the automation benefit.
  • Persistent memory agents change AI from a stateless tool into a stateful assistant — with new data governance implications. Memory stores contain user data subject to retention policies, access control, and deletion rights. Design the governance model for memory before building the capability, not after.
  • Multimodal-first architecture eliminates many preprocessing pipelines. Document intake that previously required OCR → text extraction → LLM now runs end-to-end through a vision-language model. This simplifies architecture, reduces failure modes in the preprocessing pipeline, and often improves extraction accuracy — especially for complex layouts, tables, and mixed handwritten/printed documents.
What Is It (Plain English)
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro — done at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going for architect-level detail.
Cloud platforms for AI are the managed infrastructure services that make AI capabilities available at enterprise scale — without requiring you to build GPU clusters, manage model serving infrastructure, or procure frontier model access directly.
  • AWS Bedrock: Amazon's managed LLM API layer, giving access to Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Cohere, Mistral, and Amazon Titan models through a unified API, with SageMaker for ML training and deployment
  • Azure OpenAI: Microsoft's managed deployment of OpenAI's models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, embedding models) in Azure's infrastructure, with enterprise SLAs and EU data residency options
  • Google Cloud Vertex AI: Google's unified ML platform, with access to Gemini models, custom model training, MLOps tools, and a model garden covering third-party models

For architects: choosing a cloud AI platform is partly a capability decision (which models are available, which compliance certifications are held) and partly a lock-in decision. Most enterprises choose primarily on their existing cloud provider relationship and secondarily on AI-specific capability gaps.

Why Should I Care
For Solution Architects

Cloud platform choice affects three concrete things:

  • Which models are available: Azure OpenAI has exclusive enterprise access to GPT-4o with SLAs; AWS Bedrock has Anthropic Claude; GCP Vertex has Gemini. If your use case requires a specific model, the platform follows.
  • Data residency and compliance: Azure OpenAI offers EU-hosted endpoints critical for GDPR-sensitive workloads. AWS Bedrock has region-specific endpoints. Know your data residency requirements before shortlisting platforms.
  • Integration with existing infrastructure: if your data lives in Snowflake on Azure, your pipelines run in Azure Data Factory, and your applications deploy to AKS, Azure OpenAI is lower-friction than cross-cloud connectivity to AWS Bedrock.
For Enterprise Architects

Cloud AI platforms introduce new vendor categories into enterprise procurement:

  • LLM API endpoints with per-token pricing (new cost structure, different from traditional compute)
  • Managed model serving with availability SLAs (different from self-managed model serving)
  • Data processing agreements (DPA) required for models that process personal data — each cloud provider has a different DPA template

Enterprise architects also need to track: model deprecation policies (Azure OpenAI gives 6–12 months' notice; AWS Bedrock policies vary by model provider), version pinning options, and what happens to your system if a model is retired without a suitable replacement.

Think About It Like This
The Managed Services Analogy — Before cloud computing, companies ran their own data centres: buying servers, maintaining them, managing cooling — all in-house. The cloud's value proposition was: let us manage the infrastructure so you can focus on your applications. Cloud AI platforms apply the same logic to model serving.
  • Azure OpenAI is like a managed database service — you get access to the database engine (GPT-4o) via a standard interface (REST API), in your cloud region, with enterprise SLAs, without managing the database server itself.
  • AWS Bedrock is like a managed message queue (SQS) — a choice of message queue engines (multiple LLM providers) through a unified API, with AWS handling scale, availability, and infrastructure.
  • GCP Vertex AI is the most comprehensive — more like a managed ML platform than just a model API. Google integrates training pipelines, feature stores, model monitoring, and model serving into a unified product. More powerful, but more opinionated about how you structure your ML workflows.
  • The HuggingFace Ecosystem sits alongside all three — a community hub for open-weight models, datasets, and evaluation tools, with managed inference endpoints that work across cloud providers. The model library that all three cloud platforms draw from for their open-weight model support.
Step-by-Step Walkthrough — Core Concepts
4.1 AWS Bedrock vs Azure OpenAI vs GCP Vertex: The Decision Framework

Rather than memorising every service detail (which changes quarterly), use this decision framework:

Step 1: Existing Cloud Commitment
  • Already deep in AWS (Lambda, EKS, RDS, S3)? Start with Bedrock — lower integration friction.
  • Microsoft enterprise agreement, Azure data estate? Start with Azure OpenAI.
  • GCP data warehouse (BigQuery), Kubernetes (GKE), or Google Workspace? Start with Vertex.
  • Multi-cloud or greenfield? Evaluate on capability and compliance first.
Step 2: Model Requirements
Need GPT-4o or GPT-4o-mini? → Azure OpenAI (exclusive enterprise access with SLA) → OpenAI direct API also available but no Azure-tier SLA Need Anthropic Claude? → AWS Bedrock (full Claude lineup including Opus) → Anthropic direct API also available → Azure has some Claude access but less comprehensive Need Gemini? → GCP Vertex AI (Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini Flash) → Google AI Studio for development Need open-weight (Llama, Mistral)? → All three platforms + HuggingFace Inference Endpoints + self-hosted → AWS Bedrock has strong Llama support → GCP Vertex model garden has broad selection
Step 3: Compliance & Data Residency
EU data residency required (GDPR, DPO requirement)? → Azure OpenAI EU regions (France Central, Sweden Central) → AWS eu-west-1 (Ireland), eu-central-1 (Frankfurt) → GCP europe-west (multiple) Specific compliance certifications required? Check: ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type 2, HIPAA BAA, FedRAMP All three major providers hold most enterprise certifications Verify: does the specific AI service (not just the cloud platform) hold the cert? Data never leaves your VPC? → All three offer VPC/Private Link options for API calls → Self-hosted on Kubernetes with open-weight model is most complete isolation
Step 4: Operational Integration
  • MLflow already in use? All three have MLflow integrations.
  • Terraform for infrastructure? All three have providers.
  • Existing monitoring (Datadog, Grafana)? Check native integration vs custom OTel.
4.2 AWS Bedrock: What Architects Need to Know

A managed API layer that gives access to multiple LLM providers (Anthropic, Meta, Cohere, Mistral, Amazon Titan) through a single AWS API, with unified authentication (IAM), logging (CloudWatch), and billing.

Key Architectural Features
  • Inference profiles: pre-configured model deployments with guaranteed throughput — important when you need predictable latency and don't want shared capacity fluctuations
  • Knowledge Bases for Bedrock: managed RAG infrastructure — upload documents to S3, Bedrock handles chunking, embedding, indexing, and a retrieval API layer. Reduces RAG build time; limits flexibility vs custom implementation.
  • Agents for Bedrock: managed agent runtime — define tools (Lambda functions) and orchestration logic; Bedrock handles the ReAct loop (Reason + Act pattern). Faster to set up than LangGraph; less flexible for complex state machines.
  • Guardrails for Bedrock: managed content filtering — topic blocking, PII detection, hate speech filtering. Applied at the API layer before and after model calls.
When Bedrock Is the Right Choice
  • AWS is your primary cloud
  • You need multi-provider model flexibility (today Claude, tomorrow Llama, without infrastructure change)
  • You want to use Claude without direct Anthropic API management (AWS billing, IAM auth)
  • You're building on AWS Lambda / Step Functions and want native integration

Cost note: Bedrock typically adds a small markup over direct provider API pricing. The trade-off is unified billing, AWS SLAs, and AWS-native security and compliance infrastructure.

4.3 Azure OpenAI: What Architects Need to Know

Microsoft's managed deployment of OpenAI's model family within Azure's infrastructure. Unlike direct OpenAI API: runs in your chosen Azure region, authenticates via Azure AD / Managed Identity, logs to Azure Monitor, and comes with Microsoft enterprise SLAs (99.9% uptime).

Key Architectural Features
  • Provisioned throughput: reserve capacity (measured in PTU — Provisioned Throughput Units) for predictable latency and guaranteed throughput. Eliminates shared capacity variance. Critical for production customer-facing applications.
  • Azure AI Content Safety: content moderation as a separate managed service — hate speech, violence, sexual content, self-harm classification. Can be applied to any text, not just model output.
  • Prompt flow: low-code/no-code pipeline builder for LLM workflows — useful for rapid prototyping; less suited for production engineering teams who want code-first control.
  • Azure AI Search: managed vector + keyword search service — integrates directly with Azure OpenAI for RAG patterns. Native hybrid search (vector + BM25 keyword relevance) with managed index.
When Azure OpenAI Is the Right Choice
  • Microsoft enterprise agreement in place
  • GPT-4o specifically required (best reasoning, most tested in enterprise contexts)
  • EU data residency required (strongest compliance story)
  • Azure Active Directory already the identity provider (Managed Identity auth — no API key exposure)
  • Existing Azure data estate (Azure SQL, Azure Blob, Azure Data Factory)
4.4 GCP Vertex AI: What Architects Need to Know

Google's most comprehensive AI platform — not just model API access, but a full ML lifecycle platform including training, feature stores, experiment tracking, model registry, and serving infrastructure, plus API access to Gemini models.

Key Architectural Features
  • Gemini access: Gemini 1.5 Pro (1M context window), Gemini 1.5 Flash (fast, cheap), Gemini 2.0 Pro. Gemini 1.5 Pro's 1M token context window enables full-codebase or full-document-library analysis without chunking.
  • Model Garden: curated collection of open-weight and third-party models (Llama, Mistral, Falcon, Gemma) available as managed endpoints.
  • Feature Store: managed feature store for ML — centralises feature computation, storage, and serving. Reduces train-serve skew.
  • AutoML: automated model training for tabular, text, image, and video tasks — low-code option for non-ML-engineer teams.
  • BigQuery ML: run ML models directly from BigQuery SQL — useful for data teams without Python ML expertise.
When Vertex AI Is the Right Choice
  • GCP is your primary cloud
  • Gemini's long context window (1M tokens) is required for your use case
  • You have a significant traditional ML workload alongside LLM use cases (Vertex's MLOps tooling is more comprehensive than Bedrock's)
  • BigQuery is your primary data warehouse (tight integration)
  • Google Workspace is your productivity suite (Gemini integrations across Docs, Sheets, Gmail)
4.5 The Build vs Buy vs Fine-tune Decision (Cloud Platform Lens)

Cloud platforms add a fourth option to the Build vs Buy vs Fine-tune framework: Configure.

OptionDescriptionExample
Buy (SaaS)Use a pre-built AI productMicrosoft Copilot, Salesforce Einstein, ServiceNow Now Assist
ConfigureUse cloud platform managed services (RAG, agents, guardrails) with your dataAzure AI Search + Azure OpenAI Prompt Flow
Build (custom)Build on raw model APIs with your own orchestration, evaluation, storageDirect API + LangGraph + custom eval harness
Fine-tuneAdapt a base model for your specific domain/taskQLoRA on Llama-3 for domain-specific classification
Start with Buy: is there a SaaS product that covers 80%+ of your use case? → YES: use it; build only the integration to your data/systems If not, start with Configure: do the cloud platform managed services cover your pattern? → YES: use them; custom code only where managed services don't reach If not, Build: custom implementation with direct APIs → When: managed services add unnecessary constraints; need full control Fine-tune adds to any of the above: when off-the-shelf models don't meet quality bar
🟡 Hidden cost of Configure — Managed services (Knowledge Bases, Prompt Flow, Bedrock Agents) abstract complexity but add vendor lock-in. Migrating from Bedrock Knowledge Bases to a custom Weaviate + LlamaIndex implementation is expensive engineering work. If you might need to migrate, build on portable abstractions from the start. Safe to skip on a first pass.
4.6 Technical Debt in AI Systems
Note: If you are currently evaluating which platform to choose, complete Sections 4.1–4.5 first, then return here for the long-term view.

AI systems accumulate technical debt faster than traditional software systems because:

Sources of AI Technical Debt
  • Model dependencies: code written for GPT-4 may need updating for GPT-4o; model updates are more frequent than database version updates
  • Prompt drift: prompts that worked well 6 months ago may perform worse on newer model versions. Prompts without version control and evaluation gates accumulate silent quality debt.
  • Data pipeline debt: RAG systems are only as good as their knowledge base. Stale documents, inconsistent chunking, poor metadata degrade retrieval quality over time without visible failures.
  • Evaluation debt: AI systems without evaluation harnesses have no reliable way to detect quality regressions. The longer you operate without it, the more production issues trace back to undetected model changes.
  • Governance debt: responsible AI controls added after deployment are much harder than those designed in. PII handling, audit logging, content moderation added retrospectively require touching every AI call in the codebase.
Debt Reduction Strategy

Quarterly AI system reviews covering:

  • Model version currency — are you on a deprecated version?
  • Evaluation harness completeness — are quality regressions detectable?
  • Knowledge base freshness — are indexed documents still current?
  • Governance control coverage — are all PII handling, audit logging, and content moderation controls in place?
  • Cost optimisation — have model pricing changes created better options?
Enterprise Example
Scenario: Cloud Platform Selection for a European Retail Bank

A European retail bank is deploying three AI capabilities: a customer service chatbot (2M interactions/month), an internal document assistant (50K queries/month from staff), and a credit risk narrative generator (5K reports/month, highly sensitive).

Existing infrastructure: Azure is the primary cloud (Azure SQL, Azure Blob, Active Directory). The bank holds ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type 2, and is subject to EBA guidelines on AI in banking.

Platform Evaluation
CriterionAzure OpenAIAWS BedrockGCP Vertex
EU data residency✓ France Central✓ eu-west-1✓ europe-west
GPT-4o access✓ Native✗ Not available✗ Not available
Managed Identity auth✓ Azure AD native✗ (IAM, not Azure AD)✗
Existing cloud spend✓ EA in place✗ Separate billing✗ Separate billing
Compliance (ISO 27001, SOC 2)✓✓✓
EBA AI guidelines audit support✓ MS enterprise compliancePartialPartial

Decision: Azure OpenAI — driven by existing cloud relationship, Azure AD native auth (no API key exposure risk), EU data residency, and GPT-4o access.

Architecture Decisions per Use Case
Customer Service Chatbot
ModelGPT-4o-mini (speed + cost; adequate for well-scoped customer service)
ServingProvisioned Throughput (predictable latency; shared capacity risk unacceptable for customer-facing)
KnowledgeAzure AI Search (hybrid vector + BM25 on product and policy documents)
SafetyAzure AI Content Safety applied to all outputs
Cost~£8,000/month (provisioned throughput at 200 PTU + Azure AI Search indexing)
Internal Document Assistant
ModelGPT-4o (staff tool; quality over cost)
KnowledgeAzure AI Search with SharePoint connector (indexes internal documents, policies, procedures)
AuthAzure AD SSO — staff authenticate once; no additional credentials
Cost~£300/month (100M tokens/month at £3/M; no provisioned needed at this volume)
Credit Risk Narrative Generator
ModelGPT-4o (highest quality; legal review required before output is used)
Data classificationMost sensitive tier — requires DPA signed with Microsoft (already in EA)
OutputStructured narrative + supporting citations; not used autonomously — always reviewed by credit officer
Audit trailAll prompts and completions logged to Azure Monitor with 7-year retention (EBA record-keeping requirement)
Cost~£160/month (40M tokens/month at £4/M blended)
Total platform cost: ~£8,500/month across three use cases. The bank's prior estimate for self-hosted GPU infrastructure to cover the same workloads: ~£45,000/month in capital + ops.
Architecture Perspective
The ROI Template for AI Projects

Every AI project presented to finance requires a credible ROI model. Template:

Project: [Name] Use case: [Brief description] Baseline: Current process cost: [£/month or hours/month × rate] Current quality metric: [accuracy, cycle time, error rate] AI-enabled future state: Projected process cost: [£/month] Projected quality metric: [improvement % with basis] Implementation cost (one-time): Development: [person-weeks × rate] Integration: [infrastructure setup, data migration] Training and change management: [hours × rate] Total one-time: [£] Running cost (annual): Cloud platform / API: [£/year] Maintenance (model updates, prompt management, eval): [hours × rate/year] Infrastructure: [£/year] Total annual: [£] Annual benefit: Cost saving: [£/year] Quality improvement value: [£/year, show working] Revenue impact (if applicable): [£/year, show working] Payback period: [months = one-time cost / (annual benefit - annual running cost) × 12] 3-year NPV: [calculate at your organisation's discount rate] Assumptions: [list all assumptions explicitly] Measurement plan: [exactly what metrics, what baseline, who measures, what cadence]
🟡 The most commonly missed line: Measurement plan. Without it, ROI claims are unverifiable and finance credibility is lost at the 12-month review.
Illustrative example (bank scenario from Section 5): Investment: £8,500/month managed AI services + £12,000 implementation. Efficiency gain: 3 FTE × £60,000 = £180,000/year. Quality improvement: 40% reduction in manual review errors = £45,000/year avoided cost. Payback period: ~2 months.
Check Yourself (3–5 Questions)
Self-Assessment Questions
  1. Your legal team requires that all AI processing of customer personal data occurs within the EU. Which cloud platform options satisfy this and what must be verified?

    All three major platforms have EU regions, but "EU region" is not sufficient on its own. Verify: (1) the specific AI service (not just the cloud platform) has EU-region endpoints; (2) the DPA is signed with the provider — the contractual basis under GDPR Article 28; (3) cross-region data transfer is explicitly disabled in your API configuration; (4) sub-processors: if the cloud provider uses sub-processors for the AI service, are those sub-processors also EU-compliant?; (5) logging and monitoring data also stays in the EU region. Azure OpenAI has the strongest story for European enterprises because the entire stack (compute, networking, logging, identity) stays within Azure's EU infrastructure under a Microsoft enterprise agreement.

  2. A developer proposes using AWS Bedrock's "Knowledge Bases" managed RAG service to avoid building a custom RAG pipeline. What are the advantages and trade-offs?

    Advantages: speed to deployment (hours vs weeks), maintenance reduction (no vector database to operate), native AWS integration, and iterative improvement without re-engineering. Trade-offs: (1) chunking control — managed services use opaque chunking strategies; custom content structure may require custom chunking for quality retrieval; (2) retrieval customisation — cannot implement advanced patterns like ColBERT, HyDE, or custom reranking without custom code; (3) vendor lock-in — migrating to a custom stack is significant re-engineering; (4) observability — limited per-request retrieval debugging when retrieval quality is poor on specific queries. Recommendation: use managed services for initial deployment and low-to-medium complexity use cases. Design the application layer to call the RAG endpoint via a clean interface abstraction so the underlying implementation can be swapped if needed.

  3. An architect says "standardise on GCP Vertex AI because it has the most comprehensive MLOps tooling." A developer counters "but we don't need MLOps — we're just calling LLM APIs." Who is right?

    Both are partially right. The developer is correct that for simple LLM API call patterns, traditional MLOps tooling (training pipelines, feature stores, model registries) is largely irrelevant. The architect is correct that as AI systems mature, MLOps-adjacent concerns become real: evaluation harnesses need to run on a schedule; model version tracking and regression testing apply to prompt + model version combinations; any fine-tuning planned makes a model registry and experiment tracking valuable. The resolution: evaluate on your actual requirements — which models do you need, what are your data residency requirements, what is your existing cloud commitment. MLOps tooling breadth is one factor, not the deciding one for LLM-API-first teams.

  4. Your organisation runs on AWS. A business unit wants to use Claude Opus for complex legal document analysis. How do you provide access and what governance controls do you implement?

    Access via AWS Bedrock — provides managed Claude access through IAM authentication, consolidated billing, and AWS-native logging. Steps: (1) enable Bedrock in the relevant AWS region (eu-west-1 or eu-central-1 for EU data residency); (2) create an IAM role with least-privilege Bedrock invoke permissions scoped to Claude Opus model IDs only; (3) configure VPC endpoints for Bedrock if the application runs in a private VPC; (4) enable CloudWatch logging for all Bedrock API calls. Governance controls: data classification check (legal documents likely contain sensitive information — confirm DPA covers this category and endpoint stays in required region); model version pinning (specify exact model version to prevent unexpected behaviour from provider-side updates); access logging (all invocations logged with the IAM principal for audit trail); cost controls (set AWS Budget alerts — Opus is expensive; a runaway loop would be costly); output governance (legal analysis is high-stakes — define the human review gate explicitly).

  5. What is "provisioned throughput" in Azure OpenAI or AWS Bedrock, and when should an architecture require it vs using shared capacity?

    Provisioned throughput (Azure: PTU — Provisioned Throughput Units; Bedrock: Provisioned Throughput) is a reserved compute allocation — you pay for dedicated capacity guaranteeing a specified number of tokens per minute, rather than sharing capacity with other customers. Shared capacity means your requests compete with all other customers' requests, causing during peak periods: rate limit errors (429s) requiring retry logic, latency spikes, and unpredictable performance. Provisioned throughput eliminates these. When to require it: (1) customer-facing applications with latency SLAs; (2) high-volume predictable workloads where provisioned is typically cheaper at that volume; (3) production applications where rate limit errors are unacceptable. When shared is fine: (1) internal tools, batch workloads, non-real-time use cases; (2) low-volume or bursty workloads where reserved capacity would be idle; (3) development and testing. Cost: provisioned throughput requires minimum commitments (typically monthly) regardless of actual usage — it's a fixed cost. Only makes sense when utilisation will be high enough to justify the reservation.

Advanced Deep Dive
🟡 Optional depth — This section covers SageMaker Pipelines and HuggingFace Inference Endpoints at depth. It is safe to skip on a first pass and return here when evaluating specific platform capabilities.
8.1 SageMaker Pipelines: ML Orchestration on AWS

For organisations running traditional ML (not just LLM APIs), SageMaker Pipelines provides a managed DAG orchestrator for ML workflows:

SageMaker Pipeline components: ├── Processing Step: data preprocessing (Spark or Python) ├── Training Step: model training on managed compute ├── Evaluation Step: automated metrics computation ├── Condition Step: branch on evaluation results ├── Register Step: push to Model Registry if quality passes └── Deploy Step: update endpoint if approved
Each step runs in a managed container on transient compute — no persistent infrastructure required. The pipeline is versioned in SageMaker; execution history, logs, and artifacts are tracked automatically. For LLM workflows, SageMaker Pipelines can orchestrate: dataset preparation, fine-tuning jobs, evaluation harness execution, and model registration.
8.2 HuggingFace Ecosystem: The Model Library

HuggingFace is the de facto library and hosting platform for open-weight models:

Core Components
  • Model Hub: 500,000+ models available for download — base models, fine-tuned variants, embedding models, classification models
  • Datasets: 100,000+ curated datasets for training and evaluation
  • Transformers library: the standard Python library for loading and running models
  • PEFT library: standard implementation of LoRA, QLoRA, and other parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods
  • Evaluate library: standardised evaluation metrics
  • Inference Endpoints: managed GPU hosting for your chosen HuggingFace model — no self-managed GPU required, pay-per-second
Architect Perspective

HuggingFace is the source for most open-weight models regardless of which cloud you ultimately host them on. When evaluating an open-weight model for fine-tuning or self-hosting, the model card on HuggingFace is the authoritative documentation for:

  • Base model and training data
  • Evaluation scores
  • Intended use and limitations
  • Licence terms
Key Takeaways
  • Cloud platform selection is primarily driven by existing cloud commitment and data residency requirements — not AI capability differences. Azure OpenAI for Microsoft shops with EU requirements, AWS Bedrock for AWS-first organisations, GCP Vertex for Google-heavy environments. The capability differences between platforms narrow quarterly; vendor integration and compliance are more durable differentiators.
  • "EU region" is necessary but not sufficient for GDPR compliance — verify the DPA, the specific AI service's region availability, and that cross-region data transfer is disabled. A well-intentioned EU region selection can be undermined by default cross-region logging, sub-processor chains that include US entities, or an unsigned DPA.
  • Managed RAG services (Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Azure AI Search integration) are the right starting point, not a long-term compromise — but design clean interfaces so you can migrate. Speed to deployment and reduced ops burden justify managed services. Design the application layer with an abstraction over the RAG endpoint, so the underlying implementation can be swapped without rewriting the application.
  • Provisioned throughput is mandatory for customer-facing AI applications, optional for internal tools. Shared capacity rate limits and latency spikes are acceptable for internal tools where occasional delays are tolerable. They are not acceptable for customer-facing applications where P95 latency is part of the SLA. Provision capacity before launch, not after the first customer complaint.
  • AI technical debt accumulates faster than traditional software debt — schedule quarterly AI system reviews. Model deprecation, prompt drift, stale knowledge bases, and unevaluated governance controls compound silently. A quarterly review covering model version currency, evaluation harness completeness, knowledge base freshness, and cost optimisation opportunities keeps the system from degrading invisibly.
Curated learning paths — Pick the path that matches what you actually need to ship in the next 4–8 weeks. Each path is an ordered sequence — start at #1, go down. Your progress (from the "Mark as read" button on each topic) is shown live. The next topic to read is highlighted in purple.
What is this tab? This tab is the shortcut board for role- or project-based reading sequences across the document, so you can follow a single path instead of designing your own from scratch.
Why it matters: The main plan is broad, but many users need a tighter path for one outcome such as shipping RAG quickly, learning MLOps foundations, or preparing for a frontier-model interview.
How to use it: Choose the path closest to your immediate goal, work from step 1 downward, and use the progress markers to keep your next topic visible across sessions.
Example: If you need to become productive on RAG in a weekend, this tab gives you the exact subset and order without reading unrelated areas first.
